analyses.video_title: Why Is Microsoft Stock Falling, and is it a Generational Buying Opportunity? | MSFT Stock Analysis
analyses.summary:
Parkev delivers an in-depth analysis of Microsoft's 24% year-to-date decline, exploring whether the pullback represents a generational opportunity. He methodically catalogs four interconnected headwinds before arguing that the risk/reward remains favorable.

The first and most serious headwind is OpenAI dependency. Reports that OpenAI may delay its IPO due to funding and valuation concerns directly threaten Microsoft's $627 billion remaining performance obligation backlog — several hundred billion of which is attributed to OpenAI contracts. OpenAI, which remains unprofitable, may not have the capital to honor those commitments if its fundraising stalls. Microsoft has started consciously limiting new commitments to OpenAI to reduce counterparty risk, and is diversifying its customer base. Quarterly bookings excluding OpenAI showed healthy growth; with OpenAI, bookings declined 4%.

The second headwind is memory shortage. Microsoft flagged $25 billion in incremental CapEx for 2026 attributable entirely to higher component prices (primarily memory), not additional capacity. This brings total 2026 capex to approximately $190 billion. The creator expects hyperscalers to start developing proprietary memory chips within the next 6-9 months. Windows OEM revenue is expected to decline in the high teens percent; Xbox in the low teens — both likely to worsen given PC price increases from memory costs. The third concern is competitive AI model development — Microsoft's homegrown AI model efforts lag Google's Gemini significantly. The fourth is the structural shift in Microsoft's revenue mix toward cloud services where it has less durable competitive advantage than in its legacy Windows and Office productivity suite.

Despite all this, Microsoft's revenue per employee trend is strongly upward and the company now has fewer employees than a year ago while generating $50B more in revenue. Parkev puts fair value at $519 vs a current price of $371, near its 52-week low. He rates it a top-12 buy and owns the stock.
macro_topics: OpenAI financial health and IPO risk
memory shortage impact on hyperscaler CapEx

key_points: OpenAI IPO delay raises counterparty risk — hundreds of billions in Microsoft's $627B RPO backlog is attributed to OpenAI, which is losing money
Memory shortage added $25B in incremental CapEx for 2026 to $190B total — not for more capacity, just for the same capacity at higher prices
Windows OEM revenue expected to decline high-teens%; Xbox content declining low-teens% — both likely to worsen
Revenue per employee on a steep upward slope; fewer employees than a year ago while generating $50B more revenue
AI model development significantly behind Google Gemini — a structural competitive risk for Azure AI services
Forward P/E at 22 — cheapest valuation since mid-2023 by this metric; DCF fair value $519 vs $371

key_theses: Microsoft's core competitive entrenchment in enterprise software — embedded Windows, Office, Azure — is still strong enough to weather the current AI transition challenges
The valuation gap ($371 vs $519 fair value) already prices in significant negative outcomes; risk/reward favors long-term holders
Memory shortage creating $25B in unexpected CapEx is a temporary cost headwind, not a demand problem — the capex still builds real infrastructure
warnings_or_risks: OpenAI IPO delay is the biggest near-term risk — if OpenAI's valuation collapses or it files for bankruptcy, Microsoft's RPO and revenue recognition could be severely impacted
Microsoft's AI model efforts are materially behind Google — without a competitive proprietary model after the OpenAI contract ends in 2028, this could become an existential issue
Work style shifting toward agentic AI actually favors platforms other than Microsoft's core productivity suite — the structural displacement risk is real
Conditions have worsened since late April earnings update — Windows and Xbox declines likely worse than originally guided

analyses.video_title: Should You Buy Micron Stock Before the Huge Investor Update? | MU Stock Analysis
analyses.summary:
Parkev Tatevosian addresses a common viewer question ahead of Micron's June 24, 2026 earnings report: should you buy MU stock now or wait? He begins by recapping the company's extraordinary recent performance — Micron's stock is up over 775% in the past year — and reaffirming his long-standing buy thesis, which he first published over a year ago. The catalyst has been surging AI-driven demand from data centers at a time when industry supply remains constrained, creating a pricing environment where Micron holds significant leverage.

The recent quarterly numbers are striking. Revenue came in at $23.9 billion, nearly tripling year-over-year. Gross margins expanded to 74.4% and operating margins to 67.6% — figures Parkev notes are approaching software-company territory for a manufacturer that produces physical chips entirely in-house. Free cash flow was $7 billion after $5 billion in capital expenditure. Looking ahead, management guided Q3 revenue of $33.5 billion (a ~50% sequential jump) and gross margins climbing to an unprecedented 81%.

Despite the bullish fundamental picture, Parkev raises a structural concern: semiconductors are a cyclical industry, and current peak margins make forward multiples look deceptively cheap. At 11x forward earnings and 12x forward operating cash flow — roughly half the S&P 500 average — the stock screens inexpensively, but those multiples are built on record-high denominators. He also flags a subtle management signal: with the stock near all-time highs, the team chose to pay down $4.6 billion in debt rather than execute meaningful buybacks, suggesting insiders see the valuation as stretched.

Parkev updated his DCF model to an intrinsic value of $915 per share against a market price of $1,054, placing the stock above his margin of safety threshold. He maintains a buy rating but explicitly advises against purchasing before earnings given the heightened volatility event risk and his view that downside exceeds upside at current prices after an 800% run. His recommendation: wait for the results and the post-earnings price reaction before adding exposure.

stocks.thesis: Micron is benefiting from record AI-driven demand and tight industry supply that have pushed revenue, margins, and free cash flow to all-time highs; Parkev maintains a buy rating but advises waiting until after the June 24 earnings report before initiating or adding to positions.
stocks.price_target: $915 intrinsic value (DCF); current price $1,054 above margin of safety
key_points: Revenue nearly tripled year-over-year to $23.9 billion, driven by AI data center demand and constrained memory supply
Gross margins reached 74.4% and operating margins 67.6% — exceptional for a vertically integrated manufacturer
Management guided Q3 revenue of $33.5 billion (~50% sequential growth) and gross margins climbing to 81%
Generated $7 billion in free cash flow after $5 billion in CapEx; cash from operations nearly tripled in the first six months
Management chose to pay down $4.6 billion in debt rather than buy back stock at highs — interpreted as a signal that insiders consider the valuation stretched
Trades at 11x forward P/E and 12x forward operating cash flow, but both denominators are at cyclical peak levels that are likely unsustainable
Updated DCF puts intrinsic value at $915/share; market price of $1,054 exceeds Parkev's margin of safety threshold
Despite maintaining a buy rating, Parkev explicitly recommends waiting until after the June 24 earnings report and observing the post-result price action before buying

key_theses: Micron is at a cyclical peak driven by AI demand — strong fundamentals but the boom creates unrealistic baseline expectations for valuation
The stock is fairly valued or slightly above intrinsic value at current prices; margin of safety has been consumed by the 775% rally
Management's preference for debt repayment over buybacks near all-time highs is a subtle bearish signal worth noting
Earnings on June 24 represent a high-volatility event with asymmetric downside; better to wait for the print than buy into it
Long-term buy thesis remains intact — AI-driven memory demand is structural, though growth rates will moderate as replacement cycles replace new-market expansion
warnings_or_risks: Semiconductor industry is cyclical; current supply shortages historically give way to gluts as capacity expansions across the industry come online
Forward multiples of 11x P/E look cheap but are based on peak-cycle earnings — normalized earnings would show a far higher multiple
Stock has risen ~800% in one year; high beta to any negative macro or demand shift
Management is not aggressively buying back stock at current prices, suggesting insider skepticism about sustaining current levels
Post-earnings volatility is elevated; buying before the print concentrates risk unnecessarily

analyses.video_title: How to Become A Millionaire On An Average Salary (Optimal Method)
analyses.summary:
Humphrey Yang presents a four-pillar personal finance framework for building a seven-figure net worth on a median US salary of $62,000, illustrated through the example of Alex, a fictional electrician from Columbus, Ohio. The video is an investing education piece focused on savings behavior and compound interest, not individual stock selection.

Pillar 1 (Protect the Gap) establishes that the spread between income and spending is the fundamental variable — not salary level. Humphrey notes only 2.5% of Americans have $1M+ in retirement accounts despite the math showing it's achievable on average income, because most people inflate their lifestyle rather than preserve the gap. Pillar 2 (Invest Aggressively and Consistently) uses the S&P 500 index fund as the vehicle, showing $500/month from age 25 compounding to $1.5M by 65. Starting at 35 instead of 25 results in 56% less money ($680K vs. $1.5M), illustrating the cost of delay.

Pillar 3 (Time) demonstrates compound interest's back-loaded nature: the same dollar invested grows more in year 30–34 than in the first 20 years combined. He presents data showing that missing the 10 best market days over 20 years reduces returns by 56%, and half of the best days occur during bear markets — making panic-selling the most destructive investor behavior. Pillar 4 (Avoid Fee Leakage / Capture Free Money) compares a 1% expense ratio vs. 0.05% over a 40-year timeline, showing a ~$350K difference in outcomes. He also flags employer 401k matching as 'true free money' that should never be left uncaptured.

No individual stocks are discussed. The investment vehicle throughout is a broad-based, low-cost index fund.
key_theses: Savings rate matters far more than salary — a $62K earner who saves 10% outperforms a $700K earner who saves nothing
Starting early is the single highest-leverage decision: a 10-year delay from age 25 to 35 costs over $870K by retirement
Missing the 10 best market days over 20 years destroys 56% of returns — stay invested during drawdowns
A 1% annual fee vs. 0.05% compounds to roughly $350K less over a 40-year investing horizon
Employer 401k matching is the closest thing to guaranteed free money in personal finance
warnings_or_risks: High cost-of-living areas make protecting the savings gap significantly harder on a $62K income
Lifestyle inflation is the primary wealth destroyer — every raise that goes to spending is a permanent loss of compounding power

analyses.video_title: The One Chart Signal That Calls Every Market Crash, Recession And Pullback - Gareth Soloway
analyses.summary:
Gareth Soloway delivers a macro technical analysis lesson focused on using the 50-day and 200-day moving averages across daily, weekly, and monthly timeframes as a framework for gauging market risk at different horizons. The central insight is that the 50-day MA on the daily chart is a high-probability leading indicator of near-term trend shifts: when the index breaks below it, fails to recapture it on a bounce, and rolls back over, it has historically preceded significant sell-offs. He shows this pattern played out cleanly on the NASDAQ in prior corrections.

His near-term call as of the recording: the NASDAQ has gapped below its daily 50 MA, and he is watching to see whether the current bounce can recapture it. If the index rallies to the 50 MA but gets rejected, he assigns 70–75% probability to a further drop toward ~24,000 (approximately 8% lower). This is the base case — not a crash, just a meaningful pullback to what he identifies as the prior breakout level from early-to-mid 2025.

For recession scenarios, he maps to the weekly 200 MA, which has contained every major bear market except the 2008 financial crisis and the COVID crash. His recession target on the NASDAQ sits around 17,000–18,000. The monthly 200 MA (his 'greater depression' signal, historically aligned with the dot-com bust and financial crisis) sits around 10,000 — a level he considers a tail risk for 2029–2030 but not imminent.

Macro context that colored the setup: over the weekend, the US conducted missile strikes on Iran, but by the time futures opened Sunday night, a ceasefire was announced. Gareth interprets this as evidence the current administration is deeply sensitive to stock market reactions when making geopolitical decisions — a bullish implicit backstop for equities. The week is also holiday-shortened (Friday market closure), which he notes reduces institutional participation and may limit conviction on any given move.
key_theses: NASDAQ has broken its daily 50 MA — a 70-75% probability of further downside to ~24,000 if the index fails to recapture it on the current bounce
The daily 50 MA is the near-term risk signal; the weekly 200 MA (~17,000-18,000 NASDAQ) is the recession floor; the monthly 200 MA is the depression scenario
The current administration's sensitivity to stock prices provides an implicit market backstop — policy pivots tend to happen when markets stress
Holiday-shortened week reduces institutional participation, potentially dampening volatility in either direction
warnings_or_risks: Near-term NASDAQ target of ~24,000 represents roughly 8% additional downside from the 50 MA level
If economic conditions deteriorate into a recession later in 2026, NASDAQ could reach 17,000-18,000 (weekly 200 MA)
Gareth sees a 'greater depression' scenario around 2029-2030 as a tail risk, but not in the immediate term

analyses.video_title: Buy or Sell Micron Stock? (My Final Verdict) | MU Stock Deep Dive Part 4
analyses.summary:
In the fourth and final installment of his Micron deep dive series, Parkev delivers his verdict: Hold, with relatively high conviction. The quarter was exceptional — total revenue of $41.5B (+350% YoY) with gross margins of 84.9% and an operating margin of 81.2%, which Parkev says makes Micron the highest operating profit margin business in his entire coverage universe. However, the valuation has moved with the results, and he calculates a revised DCF fair value of $1,115 vs. the current market price of $1,213 — effectively fairly valued once a 15% margin of safety is applied.

The video covers two major future growth vectors beyond the current data center cycle. First, advanced driver assistance systems (ADAS) — Level 2+ vehicles require 5x more memory than average vehicles, and such vehicles are projected to exceed 40% of production by 2030, creating a multi-year demand ramp. Second, humanoid robots are expected to carry 10x the memory of an L2 vehicle, with Micron forecasting a 'sustained substantial multi-decade memory cycle' beginning around 2030. These are speculative but genuinely incremental TAM expansions beyond the current AI infrastructure cycle.

Parkev's primary concern is that the dominant driver of this quarter's results is pricing rather than unit volume — DRAM shipments grew only low single-digit percentages YoY while prices rose 60%+, and NAND storage prices rose 80%+. He notes that price increases are expected to moderate in the upcoming quarter, and the industry has already begun signaling supply builds (two new Idaho facilities coming online mid-2027 and late-2028; Taiwan site generating shipments mid-2027). He raises the structural risk that Apple and hyperscalers may eventually develop proprietary memory/storage components — just as they moved to proprietary GPUs/TPUs — if memory pricing becomes large enough to justify the investment.

stocks.thesis: Micron delivered the strongest quarter in its history (84.9% gross margin, 81.2% operating margin, +350% revenue YoY) but at $1,213 the stock is at fair value per a revised DCF of $1,115 — hold if positioned, don't add at current prices; forward PE 19.3x is near the highest in a year.
key_points: Q3 FY2026: revenue $41.5B (+350% YoY), gross margin 84.9%, operating margin 81.2% — highest operating margin of any company Parkev covers
DCF fair value revised up 20% from $920 to $1,115 following the blowout quarter; stock at $1,213 is modestly above even the revised estimate
DRAM pricing up 60%+ YoY, NAND storage pricing up 80%+ YoY — but price increases expected to moderate in the upcoming quarter
Unit shipments grew only low single digits — the bulk of the 350% revenue increase was pricing, not volume
ADAS tailwind: Level 2+ vehicles carry 5x the memory, projected to exceed 40% of production by 2030
Humanoid robots carry 10x the memory of Level 2 vehicles; Micron projects a multi-decade memory cycle beginning ~2030
Two new Idaho facilities online mid-2027 and late-2028; Taiwan site shipments mid-2027 — supply coming
Structural risk: Apple and hyperscalers may develop proprietary memory/storage as they did with GPUs if component costs become large enough

stocks.thesis: Apple raised prices on consumer electronics due to higher Micron memory costs; Parkev raises the structural risk that Apple may eventually develop proprietary memory/storage components to avoid being squeezed by Micron, Samsung, and SK Hynix.
key_points: Apple announced meaningful price increases across its product portfolio following Micron's Q3 results
Apple's asset-light model may face re-evaluation if memory spending becomes hundreds of billions over coming years
Could follow the GPU/TPU playbook and develop proprietary memory — a structural long-term risk to Micron's hyperscaler demand

key_theses: Micron is at peak cycle economics right now — 84.9% gross margins are historically unprecedented and unlikely to persist beyond 1–2 years
Valuation has caught up to results: DCF now $1,115 vs $1,213 market price — no longer a clear buy, fairly valued
ADAS and humanoid robots are genuine incremental demand drivers beyond the current data center cycle, extending the memory supercycle into the 2030s
The pricing-driven nature of current revenue is the key risk: 60–80% YoY price increases will moderate, and the market knows this
warnings_or_risks: Price increases expected to moderate starting next quarter — the 60–80% YoY pricing tailwind is already peak-cycle
Revenue growth was mostly price, not volume — low single-digit unit growth is the structural baseline
Apple and hyperscalers may develop proprietary memory components as costs scale, threatening the largest demand pockets
Forward PE 19.3x is near the highest in the past year — valuation is stretched relative to historical norms for a cyclical business
Two new manufacturing facilities coming online 2027–2028 will add supply — the demand-supply imbalance that drives pricing will eventually correct

analyses.video_title: RocketLab Stock: My Final Verdict (Buy or Sell) | RKLB Stock Deep Dive Part 6
analyses.summary:
In the sixth and final installment of his Rocket Lab deep dive, Parkev Tatevosian examines the company's cash flow situation and delivers his investment verdict. He notes that RKLB burned roughly $75 million in free cash flow during the most recent quarter — $50 million in operating cash outflows plus $27 million in capital expenditure — and expects that elevated burn rate to persist through Neutron's first launch. Despite the losses, management sits on $1.5 billion in cash and equivalents, giving the company roughly 15 quarters of runway even at peak burn levels, and total liquidity exceeds $2 billion when including available credit facilities.

Parkev highlights that management has been opportunistically issuing equity — raising around $450 million in the latest quarter alone — while the stock has surged over 300% in the past year. He frames this favorably: each capital raise has occurred at higher prices as the company hits milestones, reducing the long-term cost of capital. For Q2 2026, management guided revenue of $225–240 million (roughly 16% above Q1's $200 million) with gross margins improving to around 34%.

The discussion then pivots to valuation in the context of SpaceX's anticipated IPO the following day. Parkev estimates SpaceX would price at a trailing price-to-sales near 90–95 at a $1.77 trillion valuation. By comparison, Rocket Lab trades at a forward P/S of approximately 62 — about two-thirds of SpaceX's multiple. He argues this relative discount is deserved given that Rocket Lab is a pure-play space company, whereas SpaceX is a conglomerate that now includes XAI (an AI company with excess computing capacity and modest customer traction) and a social media business — both of which traditionally command far lower multiples on their own.

Despite finding Rocket Lab's strategic position compelling and its valuation more attractive than SpaceX's, Parkev concludes the current price-to-sales of 62 is still too rich for him to rate the stock a buy. He speculates the SpaceX IPO could serve as a sentiment catalyst in either direction and suggests waiting for a more attractive entry point before committing capital.

stocks.thesis: Compelling pure-play space company with a strong cash runway and improving unit economics, but the current forward P/S of ~62 is too elevated relative to the risk profile; Parkev wants a lower entry point before upgrading to buy.
key_points: Burned approximately $75 million in free cash flow last quarter, with management expecting elevated spending to continue through Neutron's first launch
Holds $1.5 billion in cash and has access to over $2 billion in total liquidity, providing ample runway even at peak burn rates
Has issued equity opportunistically at progressively higher prices as it hits milestones, reducing long-term cost of capital
Q2 2026 revenue guidance of $225–240 million implies roughly 16% sequential growth, with gross margins expected near 34%
Trades at a forward P/S of ~62, roughly two-thirds of SpaceX's estimated valuation multiple, with the pure-play space focus seen as a structural advantage
SpaceX IPO (the following day) may act as a valuation catalyst — outcome could push RKLB higher or provide a better entry opportunity

key_theses: Rocket Lab has sufficient liquidity (~$2B) to reach Neutron's first launch without existential funding risk
The pure-play space positioning is more attractive than SpaceX's mixed-asset conglomerate structure at comparable or higher multiples
At a forward P/S of 62, the current price already reflects a lot of optimism; Parkev rates RKLB a hold and is waiting for a better entry
SpaceX's IPO is a near-term sentiment event that could move RKLB in either direction
warnings_or_risks: Company is burning ~$75 million per quarter in free cash flow and expects this to remain elevated
Valuation of ~62x forward P/S is historically rich and leaves little margin for execution missteps
SpaceX IPO could cause valuation compression across the space sector if enthusiasm is lower than expected
Continued equity dilution as management raises capital at market prices

analyses.video_title: Alert: This Is How Every Market Top Begins
analyses.summary:
Gareth delivers a market-top warning, arguing that the classic two-step pattern for major tops — a lower low followed by a confirmed lower high — is now developing in the S&P 500 and NASDAQ. While the indexes remain near all-time highs (NASDAQ 100 roughly 1-1.5% off), the internal breadth has deteriorated dramatically: mega-caps like Google, Amazon, Microsoft, Netflix, and Meta are all down meaningfully on the day, while only the narrow chip AI trade is holding up.

His key framework: a market top requires first a confirmed lower low (already achieved), then a confirmed lower high. The S&P last week put in what looks like a lower high, but it hasn't been confirmed yet because the prior low hasn't been retested. He emphasises that the difference between 'bullish consolidation' and a 'market top' hinges on whether that lower high holds after the next pullback tests the prior low.

On individual names, Gareth shows that Google, Microsoft, and Meta are already in clear lower-high/lower-low downtrends, with institutional sellers entering on each bounce. He uses these charts to illustrate that while the index headline numbers look benign, the underlying stocks driving past rallies are structurally weakening. Financial stocks are singled out as a pocket of relative strength, benefiting from a higher-for-longer rate environment under the new Fed chair Kevin Warsh.

On SpaceX, Gareth takes a bearish stance. The volume-weighted average price is approximately $181, meaning the average buyer is underwater. He sees a scenario where the stock bounces and triggers selling pressure from anyone who bought between $200 and $228 trying to break even. If SpaceX breaks below its IPO-day low of $149-150, every post-IPO buyer would be negative, removing any remaining buy-the-dip support.
macro_topics: Fed chair transition (Kevin Warsh) and higher-for-longer rates

stocks.thesis: SpaceX VWAP (~$181) is above the current price, meaning the average IPO buyer is underwater; any bounce will face heavy supply from trapped buyers in the $200-$228 range, and a break below the IPO-day low of $149-150 would put every post-IPO investor in the red.
key_points: Volume-weighted average price (~$181) above current price — average post-IPO buyer is now negative
Large institutional selling visible on the chart at the recent high around $228
Trapped buyers in $200-$228 range will create selling pressure on any bounce
Break below $149-$150 IPO-day low would put every public shareholder underwater, removing buy-the-dip support
Used as an example of how lower-high/lower-low patterns develop after a frothy IPO

stocks.thesis: Google is in a confirmed lower-high/lower-low downtrend; down 5.5% on the day with a clear pattern of deteriorating price action that signalled the top to anyone watching technical levels.
key_points: Down 5.5% on the day at time of recording — one of the largest single-day drops among mega-caps
Chart shows a clear sequence: high, higher high, then lower low confirmed, then lower high, lower low again
Gareth uses Google as a teaching example of how technicals flagged the top before analysts upgraded the stock
Still making lower lows — downtrend is well-established

stocks.thesis: Microsoft's bounce failed at a lower high, and the chart now shows a classic topping pattern — a useful case study of how institutional investors sold into the recovery rather than buy the dip.
key_points: Down significantly on the day alongside other non-chip tech names
Chart shows: high, lower high confirmed after a bounce, then lower low pattern
Gareth highlights Microsoft as another example where buying the dip after the lower high was a losing trade
Pattern matches prior major market tops in structure

stocks.thesis: Meta's chart has become 'sloppy' but still shows lower highs and lower lows, suggesting the big-tech rally that drove the index is losing breadth support.
key_points: Well off its highs; pattern less clean than Google or Microsoft but still directionally lower
Still making lower lows — a buy-the-dip here is a trade for a bounce, not a return to all-time highs
Pattern illustrates how breadth deterioration begins before the index rolls over

key_theses: The S&P and NASDAQ are showing early-stage market-top patterns: a confirmed lower low and what appears to be a forming lower high — confirmation comes if the prior low gets retaken
Market breadth is razor-thin: Google, Amazon, Microsoft, Netflix, Meta all declining sharply while only narrow chip AI names are holding the indexes near highs
SpaceX is a warning sign — average post-IPO buyer is already underwater, creating supply pressure on any bounce
Financial stocks are a relative-strength pocket, benefiting from higher-for-longer rate expectations under new Fed chair Kevin Warsh
warnings_or_risks: S&P breadth is historically weak near current levels — reminiscent of internals seen before dot-com, financial crisis, and pre-COVID collapses
History shows even government intervention cannot prevent major market tops once they begin (2008 example)
Buy-every-dip mentality is dangerous when lower highs are forming — anyone buying dips that then make lower lows ends up in the money briefly before losing it
SpaceX could accelerate market weakness if it breaks below $149-150, creating fear-driven selling across growth assets

analyses.video_title: Gold And Silver Forecast: One Is Closer to a Bottom Per RSI Divergences - Gareth Soloway
analyses.summary:
Gareth Soloway compares the technical setups for gold and silver, concluding that gold is closer to a tradeable bottom while silver likely has further downside ahead. He uses RSI divergence analysis as his primary tool, specifically looking for a 'three-pronged' positive divergence (three progressively lower price lows paired with three progressively higher RSI lows) as confirmation of a major bottom.

On silver: the $64 support level that had contained the price for months broke down through a 'bouncy ball' pattern — each successive bounce getting smaller until the breakdown. Silver now faces $64 as resistance rather than support, and the RSI shows no positive divergence — lower lows on price are matched by lower lows on RSI. His near-term target is $54, with a potential further flush to $50 or the high $40s. He plans to start nibbling around $54 and dollar-cost average lower, but is explicitly not chasing. On the weekly RSI, silver is not yet at the 30 (oversold) level, suggesting more downside remains.

On gold: a more intriguing setup. A wedge pattern is intact on the chart, and Gareth is beginning to see the early stages of RSI divergence forming — one positive divergence tick so far, with two more needed for his 'three-pronged' confirmation. His scenario: gold may bounce, then make one more push lower to $3,500–$3,900, with that final flush completing the triple divergence and providing a high-conviction entry. On the monthly RSI, gold reaching $3,500 would correspond to the RSI midpoint (50), a historically significant support level. Below $3,900, he plans to start buying.

Long-term, Gareth remains bullish on precious metals, citing government spending, Federal Reserve policy trajectory, and his view that the new Fed chair will ultimately prioritize rate cuts over inflation control.

stocks.thesis: Gold is forming early RSI divergence signals and one more flush to the $3,500–$3,900 range would complete a three-pronged positive divergence and create a high-conviction buy entry; long-term bullish but needs confirmation before accumulating.
key_points: Wedge pattern on the chart remains intact, with price repeatedly respecting a long-term trend line
Early single-leg RSI positive divergence forming — two more legs needed for a 'three-pronged' confirmation signal
Key entry level: below $3,900, with $3,500 as the potential downside flush target before a major bottom
Monthly RSI at $3,500 would reach the 50-midpoint level — a historically significant support zone
Long-term bullish due to government spending, Fed rate-cut bias, and inflation concerns

stocks.thesis: Silver broke its key $64 support via a 'bouncy ball' pattern with no RSI positive divergence — weekly RSI has not reached oversold levels yet, suggesting more downside; nibbling planned around $54 with full position target in the $46–$50 range.
key_points: Classic 'bouncy ball' breakdown: $64 support held but each bounce was smaller, ultimately giving way to downside
$64 now acts as resistance; recapturing it would flip the view to neutral
No positive RSI divergence: lower price lows match lower RSI lows — no exhaustion signal yet
Weekly RSI not yet at 30 (oversold), and monthly RSI needs to reach 50 for a meaningful bottom — both suggest further downside
Creator plans to start nibbling around $54, then dollar-cost average toward $50 and possibly $46–$48

key_theses: Gold is closer to a tradeable bottom than silver — early RSI divergence is forming, and one more flush to $3,500–$3,900 would create a high-conviction entry
Silver has not yet reached oversold RSI levels on weekly or monthly timeframes — more downside likely before accumulation makes sense
Long-term bullish on both metals due to fiscal spending and a Federal Reserve structurally inclined toward rate cuts
Emotional extremes at lows and highs are the enemy of good entry points — wait for RSI divergence confirmation, not price level alone
warnings_or_risks: Silver could fall to $50 or even the high $40s before finding a sustainable bottom
Gold's wedge trend line has weakened due to multiple touches — a clean break below could accelerate the move to $3,500
The 'three-pronged divergence' signal may take weeks or months to fully develop, requiring patience before full position sizing

analyses.video_title: The Biggest Market Catalyst This Week + Charts I'm Watching
analyses.summary:
Josh gives a technical market overview covering SPY's historic run, the week's biggest catalyst (Thursday's jobs report), and charts on Nike, Tesla, and SpaceX. His primary thesis is bullish with a caveat: the SPY has now run 8 straight six-month green candles from $324 to $757 — an historically unusual streak. Comparing to the dot-com era (11 straight green candles), he argues there may still be 1.5 years of upside if the AI bubble follows the dot-com playbook, but that when the run ends, a 50% drawdown is historically typical.

His immediate market read is bullish — SPY broke above the key 743 resistance level he flagged the previous day, confirming bulls are in control. He expects Wednesday to be a consolidation day before Thursday's jobs report, which he frames as 'bad news is good news' — a soft report reduces Fed hawkishness pressure. He is watching for SPY to potentially reach 950 over the next year and a half.

On specific stocks: RKLB called out his prior buy zone at $80 (hit and ripped to $100+); he is holding shares with a stop-loss. TSLA had a breakout through 411, now watching 425.50 as next resistance and 450 as the target above that. SPCX consolidated at 161, bounced to 168, he targets 176 as near-term resistance. Nike (NKE) monthly chart shows the longest monthly downtrend in the stock's history — RSI deeply oversold on monthly timeframe — he sees potential short-squeeze setup but won't predict earnings direction.

stocks.thesis: SPY broke above the key 743 resistance confirming near-term bullish control; dot-com comparison suggests potential for another 1.5 years of upside to ~950, but the eventual end of this run will likely produce a ~50% drawdown that most investors are not prepared for.

Gave Discord members a stop-loss target on RKLB

analyses.video_title: Alert: Crypto Late Innings Bear Market, Major Breakout Potential In Alts (BTC, ETH, SOL, XRP)
analyses.summary:
Gareth Soloway provides a crypto technical analysis update, concluding that Bitcoin is in 'Stage 2' of its bear market — the late innings — while select altcoins (Ethereum, Solana, XRP) are showing relative strength and potential wedge breakouts. His framework distinguishes two stages of a bear market: Stage 1 is where price is below the main trend line from the ATH, and Stage 2 is where price has broken back above that trend line but is still declining. Being in Stage 2 means the bear market is 'closer to the end than the beginning' in time, though more downside remains.

Bitcoin closed June 30 at approximately $58,500 — its lowest close of the recent period. Gareth attributes some of this pressure to end-of-quarter 'window undressing' — institutions dumping underperforming assets (Bitcoin, Microsoft) to clean up their quarterly statements while buying outperformers (Micron, SanDisk). He expects this selling pressure to abate at the start of Q3 and sees a potential relief rally in Bitcoin before a final flush to the $50,000 area that would mark the end of the bear market.

On altcoins: ETH has not made a new low vs Bitcoin's new low — relative strength divergence. ETH is in a wedge pattern that must resolve by approximately July 17 as the lines converge. Solana has held above its lows and broke out from a trend line — Gareth is long Solana with a target of $100 if the $76–77 trend line breaks. XRP is in a converging wedge that also must resolve by around July 17. He frames both ETH and XRP as binary setups — big move coming in either direction.

stocks.thesis: Bitcoin is in Stage 2 of its bear market (late innings but not over) — closed at ~$58,500 (recent low), end-of-quarter selling pressure may abate in Q3, but next downside target is ~$50,000 before a potential bear market bottom.
stocks.price_target: ~$50,000 (next downside target / potential bear market bottom)

If key support breaks, next flush target is the low $50s to $50,000 range
Potential relief rally first (especially if altcoins break out), then final Bitcoin flush to ~$50K for bear market bottom

stocks.thesis: ETH has not made a new low while Bitcoin did — relative strength signal; wedge pattern must resolve (up or down) by approximately July 17; breakout could produce significant upside, breakdown targets prior lows.
key_points: Not making new lows while Bitcoin did — relative strength divergence vs BTC
Wedge pattern with parallel trend lines converging by ~July 17 — big move coming in one direction
Breakout above wedge resistance = significant upside; breakdown = retest of prior lows

key_points: Gareth explicitly long Solana as of this video
Well off its lows — relative strength signal vs Bitcoin and broader crypto
Break above $76–77 trend line = target $100

End-of-quarter window undressing artificially pressured Bitcoin on June 30; AI/chip stocks benefited from the same rebalancing flow; this rotation should reverse at Q3 start
XRP and ETH wedge patterns must resolve by ~July 17 — one of the cleanest binary setups in the crypto space currently
warnings_or_risks: Bitcoin Stage 2 bear still has downside — $50,000 target means another ~14% decline from $58,500
Wedge patterns in ETH and XRP can break in either direction — not a directional trade until the break occurs
Michael Saylor/Strategy forced selling risk continues to pressure Bitcoin specifically as a concentration overhang

analyses.video_title: Should Investors Buy ServiceNow Stock Instead of Adobe Stock? | NOW Stock Analysis | ADBE Stock
analyses.summary:
Parkev pits ServiceNow against Adobe in a head-to-head comparison of two AI-fear-beaten-down software stocks. Both are trading at the cheapest valuations in their respective histories, and Parkev concludes that both are significantly undervalued — though for different reasons and with different risk profiles.

ServiceNow is the clear growth winner: analysts project 20% annual revenue growth for NOW versus roughly 10% for ADBE. ServiceNow has also shown exceptional improvement in operating cash flow margin (from ~8% in 2017 to 39% today) and return on invested capital (from -50% to 12.7%). Despite this, it trades at a forward P/E of 23.6, its lowest ever, which Parkev finds attractive. His DCF fair value for NOW is ~$158 versus the current ~$97 — a 63% upside.

Adobe is the profitability winner on an absolute basis: 41.6% cash flow margin, 39.6% return on invested capital (more than three times ServiceNow's), and a forward P/E of just 8.2 — the cheapest in Adobe's history. His DCF fair value for ADBE is ~$380 versus the current ~$200, representing roughly 90% upside. The investor fear around Adobe centers on AI creative tools (Midjourney, Firefly, Sora) disrupting its Creative Suite dominance, but Parkev notes there is no evidence yet of significant revenue impairment.

The decisive differentiator is management. ServiceNow's CEO is widely regarded as exceptional. Adobe's CFO is departing to Marvell, and its CEO has also announced departure with no successor named yet — a meaningful governance risk at a critical inflection point. Despite this, Parkev's ultimate recommendation is Adobe by a small margin due to deeper valuation discount and superior absolute profitability. He notes this is an unusual situation where he already owns Adobe and would personally buy ServiceNow to diversify — but if starting fresh with no position in either, he picks Adobe.

stocks.thesis: Adobe trades at a forward P/E of 8.2 — its cheapest ever — and a DCF fair value of ~$380 versus the current ~$200, while showing 39.6% ROIC and 41.6% operating cash flow margins; the AI disruption narrative has over-punished the stock ahead of any material revenue evidence.
key_points: Forward P/E of 8.2 is the cheapest in Adobe's history — priced like a declining business despite ~10% annual revenue growth forecasts
DCF fair value of ~$380 implies roughly 90% upside from current $200 price
ROIC of 39.6% and cash flow from operations margin of 41.6% — best-in-class profitability metrics
No material evidence yet of AI competitors meaningfully eroding revenue, but the market has priced in significant damage preemptively
CEO departure announced, CFO leaving for Marvell — leadership vacuum is the primary near-term risk
Creator's preferred pick between the two, though he already owns it; would buy ServiceNow for diversification

stocks.thesis: ServiceNow trades at a forward P/E of 23.6 — its cheapest in years — with a DCF fair value of ~$158 vs the current ~$97, and is the superior growth story with 20% projected revenue growth versus Adobe's 10%, led by one of the best-regarded CEOs in software.
key_points: Forward P/E of 23.6 is the lowest the stock has been available for in recent history
DCF fair value of ~$158 implies roughly 63% upside from current ~$97 price
Revenue growth expected at ~20% annually — more than double Adobe's ~10% pace
Operating cash flow margin improved from ~8% in 2017 to 39% today — exceptional operational leverage trend
CEO considered one of the best in the software industry — significant management quality advantage over Adobe currently
ROIC of 12.7% is well below Adobe's 39.6% on an absolute basis, though improving strongly

stocks.thesis: Mentioned only as the destination for Adobe's departing CFO — no analysis of the business provided.
key_points: Adobe's CFO announced departure to join Marvell

key_theses: Both Adobe and ServiceNow are trading at historically cheap valuations driven by AI disruption fear that has outrun the actual revenue evidence
Adobe offers deeper value on a DCF basis (~90% upside) and superior profitability; ServiceNow offers superior growth (~20% vs 10%) and better management stability
If forced to choose one: Adobe by a small margin (deeper discount + profitability), but both merit portfolio inclusion
Adobe's leadership vacuum (CEO searching, CFO leaving) is the primary near-term headwind and the key reason the pick is 'small margin' rather than decisive
warnings_or_risks: Adobe has no named CEO successor — governance uncertainty at a critical inflection point for AI strategy
AI creative tools (image generation, video generation) are the most direct competitive threat to Adobe's Creative Suite and could accelerate revenue erosion
ServiceNow's valuation premium (3x Adobe on forward P/E) could compress further if growth slows or AI disrupts enterprise workflow automation

analyses.video_title: Nu Holdings Stock Analysis: Buy or Sell? | NU Stock Analysis
analyses.summary:
Parkev Tatevosian revisits Nu Holdings after a painful year-to-date decline of approximately 24%, addressing whether the pullback constitutes a buying opportunity. He opens by contextualizing the underperformance: multiple investment-bank downgrades from Bank of America (to underperform), Citigroup, and Susquehanna have pressured the stock, each citing leadership changes and mounting credit concerns driven by rising 30-, 60-, and 90-day loan delinquency rates.

Parkev emphasizes that defaults in Latin American consumer lending carry a higher loss-given-default than secured lending categories because there is limited collateral to recover. He notes, however, that management retains meaningful levers — curtailing new loan origination or selling debt to third parties — to manage cash flows if conditions deteriorate further.

On the positive side, the company has maintained an operating-cash-flow-to-sales ratio around 41% consistently for four years, and the forward price-to-earnings multiple has compressed to roughly 13.9x, near multi-year lows. His discounted cash flow model places fair value near $23.70 against a current price around $12.66, suggesting the stock trades at a deep discount. He acknowledges the inherent difficulty in valuing lending businesses but reiterates a buy rating, which he formally reasserted on June 16, 2026.

Parkev also flags a macro headwind: investor attention in emerging markets has rotated toward Southeast Asian semiconductor companies tied to the AI wave, leaving Latin American fintech comparatively neglected. He views this sentiment discount as part of what creates the opportunity.

stocks.thesis: Deep valuation discount (forward P/E ~13.9, DCF fair value ~$23.70 vs $12.66 price) makes the dip a buying opportunity despite legitimate credit quality concerns and bank downgrades.
key_points: Stock down ~24% year-to-date in 2026, underperforming broader market
Downgrades from Bank of America (underperform), Citigroup, and Susquehanna citing leadership and credit risks
Rising 30/60/90-day loan delinquency rates; high loss-given-default in LatAm markets due to limited collateral
Management can tighten lending or sell debt to third parties to manage cash flow if needed
Operating cash flow to sales ~41% maintained for four consecutive years
Forward P/E near 13.9x, close to the lowest level since at least January 2025
Current price ~$12.66, near 52-week low of $11.20; analyst's DCF fair value ~$23.70
Buy rating formally reiterated on June 16, 2026

key_theses: Nu Holdings' 24% YTD drawdown is primarily sentiment-driven (bank downgrades, AI-rotation away from LatAm) rather than fundamental collapse
Credit quality is a real but manageable risk given management's cash-flow levers
Valuation at ~13.9x forward earnings with a DCF fair value nearly double the current price provides meaningful margin of safety
Investor attention rotating to Southeast Asian AI plays has created a temporary discount in LatAm fintech
warnings_or_risks: Rising loan delinquency rates (30/60/90-day) with high loss-given-default in LatAm consumer lending
Multiple analyst downgrades signal institutional concern about credit trajectory
Lending-company cash flows are inherently volatile and harder to value accurately
Macro headwind: global capital flowing toward AI and Southeast Asian semiconductors, deprioritizing Latin American markets

analyses.video_title: A 10% Flush… Opportunity in July
analyses.summary:
Stocks with Josh leads with a promotional segment for his Discord/tools tier before transitioning into technical analysis of Tesla and Nike. The promotional content describes recent successful options trades including PLTR calls (+150%) and INTC puts (+110%).

On Tesla: Q2 deliveries came in at over 480,000 vehicles — well above Wall Street estimates of 400,000-460,000 — representing one of Tesla's strongest delivery beats in years. Despite the strong result, the stock flushed 10-11% on a sell-the-rip dynamic, which the creator characterizes as algo-driven. He presents historical data suggesting that after a 10%+ single-day drop that is NOT driven by bad news, Tesla has been higher 30 days later 85% of the time, and higher 60 days later 95% of the time. Key technical levels: support at $381.68, critical bear-case breakdown at $369, and an initial bounce target of $411. He was long puts on the rejection at $425 (which he had flagged as a key resistance level) and is now leaning toward going long next week.

On Nike: Josh ran a successful trade from below $42 to $47, then called rangebound action and waited for a pullback back to $41. He re-entered at $41 through earnings (which were mixed but sold off), bought the dip to $37, and is now holding with a stop-loss in place. His target is $47 initially, then $53 on a breakout above $47. He describes this as his biggest trade of the past couple weeks.
macro_topics: sell-the-news dynamics on strong earnings beats

stocks.thesis: Q2 deliveries crushed expectations at 480K+ vs ~430K estimate, but stock sold off 10% on sell-the-rip dynamics; historical data shows 85% probability of being above today's low in 30 days after similar algo-driven flushes on good news.
key_points: Q2 deliveries over 480,000 — one of Tesla's strongest beats in years against estimates of 400-460K
10%+ flush was an algo-driven sell-the-news event, not a fundamental deterioration signal
Historical pattern: after a 10%+ drop on good news, Tesla is higher 30 days later 85% of the time and 60 days later 95% of the time
Key support at $381.68; if $369 breaks, the next bear target would be $338
Creator turning to long bias for next week after successfully trading puts from $425 rejection

stocks.thesis: Successfully traded from $41-42 range through a volatile earnings sell-off to $37, bought the dip, and now positioned long with target of $47 then $53 on a breakout above $47.
key_points: Successfully traded the $42-to-$47 range, called the pullback, re-entered at $41 before earnings
Earnings sold off to $37 but creator bought the dip — held with stop-loss in place
Stock squeezed back above $42.60; creator expects retest of $47 and eventual break to $53
Technical thesis: $47 is first resistance, second pass through $47 expected to break to $53

analyses.video_title: Bitcoin at 59K: Break or Bounce? (Plus 9 Altcoins) - Gareth Soloway Forecast And Technical Analysis
analyses.summary:
Gareth Soloway covers Bitcoin and nine altcoins following a flush to ~$59K that bounced after Micron's strong earnings report. His framework is that the AI trade is simultaneously a tailwind (positive market sentiment) and a headwind (sucking capital away from crypto) for Bitcoin. The Micron earnings snap-back temporarily lifted crypto but doesn't resolve the structural question of whether crypto can hold key support.

On Bitcoin: the key binary is $67,200 resistance vs. $59,000 support. A daily close below $59K targets the low-$50K to $50K zone. A rally through $67,200 opens the path to $73K–$75K. Gareth bought some ETH at $1,500 during the dip. He believes a catalyst for a crypto bounce could come from Trump administration policy moves targeting crypto voters ahead of midterms, potentially between August and October.

On Micron specifically: despite the strong earnings beat, Gareth is cautious because SK Hynix is listing in the US (selling $29B worth) on July 10th, offering an alternative for investors. SK Hynix trades at a lower valuation than Micron. He also notes Micron's 86% margins will likely compress as competing memory comes online and tech companies build internal alternatives. He compares it to Nvidia's margin trajectory.

Altcoin summary: Ethereum (neutral, watching $1,580 support — break targets $1,400); Solana (positive, trend line holding); XRP (bullish if breaks $1.16 — could see large move); Avalanche (cautiously positive, holding recaptured trend line above $6.38); Chainlink (neutral, double bottom holding); one unnamed altcoin showed a strong bull flag breakout; NEAR Protocol (neutral, watching $1.83 support).
macro_topics: AI trade vs. crypto capital flows
Memory chip competition from SK Hynix

stocks.thesis: Strong earnings beat lifted the stock but Gareth is cautious: SK Hynix listing in the US on July 10th ($29B offering) gives investors a lower-valuation alternative; Micron's 86% gross margins will compress as competing capacity comes online, similar to Nvidia's trajectory.
key_points: Beat earnings and raised guidance; stock rebounded ~16% helping lift crypto markets
SK Hynix listing US shares July 10th ($29B offering) at lower valuation than Micron — sucks capital away
Micron's 86% gross margins will compress as new production capacity and internal memory projects come online

Break above $1.16 could be parabolic — potential 50%+ move

analyses.video_title: This Shouldn't Be Possible...
analyses.summary:
New Money's creator breaks down the anatomy of Elon Musk's newly achieved trillion-dollar net worth following SpaceX's IPO on June 12, 2026. He explains that the wealth is concentrated almost entirely in two companies: SpaceX (approximately 42-43% economic stake, valued at roughly $850 billion post-IPO listing and constituting about 75% of total net worth) and Tesla (approximately 20% economic interest worth roughly $280 billion, including options from the landmark 2018 compensation package reinstated by the Delaware Supreme Court in December 2025).

The creator methodically addresses why this trillion-dollar figure is largely inaccessible in practice. First, any meaningful liquidation would create selling pressure and signal a loss of founder confidence, depressing the very asset being sold. Second, SpaceX's IPO imposes a 366-day lock-up period through approximately June 13, 2027, legally barring Musk from selling the shares. Third, nearly 75% of the portfolio sits in a single recently-listed company — textbook concentration risk.

He then reveals the workaround used by ultra-high-net-worth individuals: pledging shares as collateral to borrow cash, avoiding both a taxable event and market selling pressure — though at the risk of margin calls in a downturn. The creator closes with the thesis that for Musk, the endgame was never liquidity — it is voting control over SpaceX and Tesla to pursue his long-term missions including a Mars colony, which is literally a condition for some of his SpaceX performance-share vesting tranches.

The creator is explicitly skeptical of the SpaceX valuation, stating he considers it massively overvalued and places his own fair value around $440 billion versus the $1.75 trillion+ IPO price, suggesting Musk's true net worth could be far lower if market sentiment corrects.

stocks.thesis: Tesla is discussed solely as a component of Musk's net worth (~$280B stake including 2018 options package), with no investment recommendation made for public investors.
key_points: Musk holds approximately 20% economic interest in Tesla including options
2018 compensation package (304 million shares via options) was reinstated by Delaware Supreme Court in December 2025 and registered for exercise in April 2026
Tesla stake worth ~$280 billion; represents roughly 25% of Musk's total net worth
No buy or sell recommendation made for the stock

stocks.thesis: Creator believes SpaceX IPO'd at a 'massively overvalued' price of $1.75 trillion, with his own fair-value estimate around $440 billion — implying roughly 75% downside from IPO valuation if sentiment normalizes.
stocks.price_target: $440B market cap (creator's bear case fair value)
key_points: SpaceX IPO'd on June 12, 2026 at a ~$1.75 trillion valuation
Musk holds ~42-43% economic stake (4.8B shares + 350M options) worth ~$850B at listing
X/xAI/Grok merged into SpaceX prior to IPO; $250B xAI valuation now embedded in SpaceX price
Creator's own valuation of SpaceX is approximately $440 billion — far below the $1.75T+ market price
366-day lock-up period prevents Musk from selling until approximately June 13, 2027
Additional 1 billion performance-based restricted shares vest in 15 tranches tied to market cap milestones up to $7.5T and a Mars colony of 1 million people

key_theses: Musk's trillion-dollar net worth is almost entirely illiquid ownership in SpaceX and Tesla, not accessible cash
SpaceX's $1.75 trillion IPO valuation is speculative and the creator considers it massively overvalued relative to fundamentals
The lock-up period, liquidity constraints, and concentration risk make Musk's paper wealth highly fragile to sentiment shifts
The real motivation behind accumulating ownership is voting control to pursue long-term missions, not wealth realization
warnings_or_risks: SpaceX trading at roughly 4x the creator's fair value estimate — significant valuation risk for public investors
75% of Musk's wealth concentrated in one recently-listed IPO — extreme concentration risk
Any large insider selling would signal lack of conviction and trigger further selling pressure
Margin calls on pledged shares could force fire-sale liquidation at the worst possible time

analyses.video_title: This Chart Lines Up Eerily With 1929, Great Depression Coming?
analyses.summary:
Gareth Soloway presents a macro bear case built around a 100-year cycle comparison between the 1920s and today. He argues that multiple structural parallels — post-pandemic boom, transformative technology euphoria, retail speculation, elite information weaponization, circular capital flows among mega-caps, and extreme market concentration — closely mirror the conditions of the late 1920s ahead of the 1929 crash and Great Depression.

Soloway draws specific parallels: the Spanish flu of 1918 maps to COVID-19 in 2020 as the demand catalyst; electrification and the automobile map to AI and cloud infrastructure; bucket shops and margin speculation map to zero-day options and prediction markets; and 1920s utility holding companies recycling capital internally maps to today's mega-cap tech firms investing in AI startups that immediately route funds back to buy cloud and chips from the same parent companies.

His chart analysis centers on the Dow Jones logarithmic chart, where he identifies a long-term parallel channel. He notes the market currently sits above the upper band of this channel — the same position it was in just before the 1929 crash. Using a date-range measurement, he calculates that approximately 3,000 days elapsed from the post-Spanish flu bull market start to the 1929 top. Applying the same 3,000-day window from the COVID low in 2020 projects a potential market top around mid-to-late 2028, leading into a 2029 crash — the 100-year anniversary. He is explicit that this is speculative and markets could see further 20–30% corrections along the way before any final top.

Soloway stops short of making any specific stock recommendations. The video is a macro warning framework, not a trade idea, and he acknowledges the scenario may not materialize.
key_theses: The current market environment has striking structural similarities to the late 1920s — post-pandemic boom, transformative tech, retail speculation, and extreme index concentration
A logarithmic Dow chart shows the market above its long-term upper channel band, the same position it occupied just before the 1929 crash
Date-range analysis from COVID low (2020) projects a potential cyclical top around mid-to-late 2028, followed by a 2029 crash — the 100-year cycle
Extreme concentration in 10–15 AI-related stocks masks underlying market weakness; advance-decline breadth is poor
US debt trajectory ($4T+ annual additions, compounding) becomes unsustainable by the 2028–2029 window
warnings_or_risks: Gareth is explicit this is speculative — the cycle may not materialize or could arrive later
Markets can continue rallying 20–30% from here before any major top
Circular capital flows among mega-cap tech firms (investing in AI startups that immediately buy cloud/chips back) may be creating synthetic demand that overstates real earnings power
Zero-day options and retail prediction platforms have increased systemic fragility relative to historical norms
Federal Reserve money printing and government deficit spending may delay but ultimately worsen the eventual correction

analyses.video_title: Should You Buy Nike Stock Before the Huge Investor Update? | NKE Stock Analysis
analyses.summary:
Parkev delivers a cautious pre-earnings take on Nike ahead of its June 30th quarterly report, concluding he would not buy the stock before the results. Nike is down approximately 29% year-to-date in 2026, and Parkev says he had warned this would happen given the headwinds and overvaluation entering the year.

The most recent quarter (ended February 28) showed flat revenue at $11.3 billion, down 3% on a currency-neutral basis — a stark contrast to Nike's historical high single-digit to low double-digit growth. Gross margins fell 130 basis points to 40.2%, and EBIT margins declined to 5.6% for the quarter from 7.3% a year prior, with the nine-month figure also materially worse at 7.1% versus 9.9%. Tariffs and rising trade barriers are the primary culprit for margin compression.

Current CEO Elliott Hill's wholesale rebuilding strategy is showing only nascent progress. Wholesale revenues grew 5% (1% currency-neutral) to $6.5 billion, but Nike Direct suffered — digital down 9% and owned stores down 5%. Parkev acknowledges the strategy direction is sound but questions whether it's working fast enough, flagging the risk of another strategic pivot in coming quarters if momentum doesn't accelerate.

On valuation, Parkev's revised DCF places Nike's fair value at $36.21, well below the current ~$45 price, making the stock overvalued even after a 29% decline. He explicitly says he wants to own Nike but not at these prices, and is waiting either for a meaningfully lower price or for evidence the turnaround is gaining traction.
macro_topics: Tariff and trade barrier impact on apparel margins

stocks.thesis: Nike's fundamentals have deteriorated faster than its stock price has fallen — EBIT margins at multi-year lows, revenue declining, and DCF fair value at $36.21 versus a $45 market price makes this a stock to watch but not buy ahead of earnings.
key_points: Reports earnings after close on June 30, 2026; stock down ~29% YTD
Last quarter: flat revenue at $11.3B, down 3% currency-neutral — vs. historical high-single-digit growth
Gross margin fell 130 bps to 40.2%; EBIT margin dropped to 5.6% from 7.3% a year ago
Wholesale revenues up 5% (1% currency-neutral) — early signs of rebuilding damaged retailer relationships
Nike Direct declining: digital down 9%, Nike-owned stores down 5%
CEO Elliott Hill executing a wholesale-rebuild strategy; direction clear but pace very slow
Tariffs and trade barriers cited as primary margin headwind across the full industry
DCF fair value revised down to $36.21 vs. ~$45 current price — overvalued even after 29% decline
Would not buy before earnings; waiting for lower price or clearer turnaround evidence

stocks.thesis: Mentioned briefly as a comparison point — Lululemon also saw gross margins fall, more sharply than Nike, though from a higher base; no investment recommendation made.
key_points: Gross margins fell more sharply than Nike's in the recent quarter, though from a higher base
Mentioned only as industry context — no buy/sell view expressed

key_theses: Nike is not a buy before earnings — the stock is overvalued relative to deteriorating fundamentals even after a 29% decline
The wholesale-rebuild strategy under CEO Elliott Hill is directionally sound but progressing too slowly to justify the current valuation

Waiting for either a much lower price or concrete turnaround evidence before taking a position
