Tariff environment directly compressing gross margins with no near-term resolution visible
DCF fair value $36.21 still below market price at $45 after a major drawdown

analyses.video_title: Is UnitedHealth Stock an Undervalued Healthcare Stock to Buy? | UNH Stock Analysis
analyses.summary:
Parkev provides an updated buy rating on UnitedHealth at $408, though with notably less enthusiasm than six to nine months ago. UNH is up over 23% in 2026 following a management-led recovery from last year's margin collapse, and Parkev views the current price as still reasonable but with reduced upside.

The backstory: UNH's margins collapsed in 2025 after management underestimated cost-to-serve customers. Operating margins fell from 8.1% to 4.2% and OCF/sales from 7.8% to 4.2%. Management responded by raising premiums and exiting unprofitable markets entirely — actions investors have rewarded with a 23%+ recovery.

Over the past month, several negative catalysts have created headwinds: Fairview Health Services terminated its Medicare Advantage relationship with UNH (affecting 11,000 patients), Arizona AG filed a price-fixing lawsuit, and Berkshire Hathaway fully divested its stake. Parkev acknowledges the Berkshire exit creates a negative market signal — investors tend to follow Buffett's successors — but does not view any individual item as a thesis-changer.

Valuation has risen to a forward P/E of 21.5x and P/OCF of 15.2x — at the higher end of historical ranges. His updated DCF pegs fair value at $469 against a current price of ~$408. He maintains the buy rating on June 17, 2026 but frames UNH primarily as a low-volatility portfolio diversifier rather than a high-upside play. Like LLY, he recommends it specifically for its low correlation to the macro economy and the AI trade.
macro_topics: Portfolio diversification away from AI trade

stocks.thesis: UNH is still undervalued at $408 with a DCF fair value of $469, but conviction is lower than earlier in 2026; management's pricing actions and market exits have stabilized margins and the stock offers low macro correlation as a portfolio diversifier.
key_points: Stock up 23%+ YTD after management raised premiums and exited unprofitable Medicare Advantage markets
Operating margin collapsed from 8.1% to 4.2% in 2025; management actions are working to restore them
Fairview Health terminated Medicare Advantage relationship (11,000 patients affected)
Arizona AG filed a price-fixing lawsuit against UNH
Berkshire Hathaway fully divested its entire UNH stake — historically a negative market signal
Trading at forward P/E of 21.5x and P/OCF of 15.2x — higher end of historical ranges
Fair value $469 vs. current $408; near 52-week high of $416

key_theses: UNH remains a buy at $408 but with reduced upside and lower conviction versus earlier in 2026
Healthcare stocks are macro-uncorrelated diversifiers — especially valuable for AI-heavy portfolios
Management's pricing discipline and market exits are working: operating margins are recovering
First and second healthcare stock added to a portfolio provides the largest diversification benefit
warnings_or_risks: Berkshire Hathaway full exit creates persistent negative sentiment overhang
Arizona price-fixing lawsuit adds legal/regulatory risk
Medicare Advantage disruptions (Fairview exit) could signal broader cost pressures returning
Valuation at the high end of historical ranges limits margin of safety

analyses.video_title: What's Going on With Micron Stock? | MU Stock Deep Dive Part 1
analyses.summary:
Parkev delivers a post-earnings deep dive into Micron's fiscal Q3 2026 results, which blew past all three components of the company's own guidance — revenue, gross profit margin, and EPS each came in above the high end. The standout figure is data center SSD revenue exceeding $5 billion, more than doubling from the prior quarter, driven by hyperscaler demand that continues to dramatically outpace industry supply. Management's language has also shifted: where three months ago they guided demand exceeding supply through 2026, they now expect tight conditions to persist well beyond calendar year 2027.

Parkev frames this as a structural, not cyclical, shift. Supply cannot keep pace because semiconductor manufacturing capacity takes years to add, and industry participants — Micron, SK Hynix, Samsung, TSMC — have deliberately refrained from over-investing given historical cyclicality. Meanwhile, AI system performance is now architecturally memory-dependent in a way it never was before, giving Micron greater differentiation than at any prior point in its history. Year-over-year DRAM price increases of 60% in the most recent quarter reflect this dynamic.

A notable second-order effect: Apple announced price increases on consumer electronics the morning after Micron's earnings, and Parkev explicitly connects this to higher memory and storage component costs flowing through Apple's supply chain. AAPL shares fell over 5% on the day as a result. This illustrates how Micron's pricing power is rippling across the entire consumer electronics ecosystem.

The bullish thesis is reinforced by 16 strategic multi-year customer agreements signed by Micron, covering data centers, consumer electronics, and automotive customers, locking in supply commitments through 2030. Parkev sees this as a fundamental business model transformation that reduces the boom-bust cyclicality Micron has historically suffered. He closes noting the stock rose 16-17% on the day of the earnings release and teases a Part 2 covering additional details.

stocks.thesis: Demand for Micron's memory and storage products significantly exceeds supply and is expected to remain that way past 2027, driving 60% YoY DRAM price increases and a business model transformation toward multi-year strategic agreements that reduce cyclicality.
key_points: Q3 FY2026 earnings beat on revenue, gross margin, and EPS — all above the high end of guidance
Data center SSD revenue topped $5 billion, more than doubling quarter-over-quarter
Management extended their tight-supply outlook from 2026 to beyond calendar year 2027
Signed 16 strategic multi-year customer agreements covering DRAM and NAND volumes through 2030
60% year-over-year DRAM price increases achieved in the most recently completed quarter
AI performance is now architecturally dependent on memory, giving Micron greater differentiation than ever before

stocks.thesis: Apple is being squeezed by higher memory and storage component costs from Micron and the broader AI infrastructure cycle, forcing price increases on consumer electronics that weighed on the stock more than 5% on the day.
key_points: Announced consumer electronics price increases the morning after Micron's Q3 earnings report
Stock fell over 5% as investors priced in margin compression from higher memory costs
Higher memory prices flowing through nearly every device Apple sells to consumers

key_theses: Micron's Q3 results were exceptional across every metric — this is not a one-quarter beat but a sustained, multi-year demand story
AI has made memory architecturally critical in a way that gives Micron pricing power it has never had historically
The 16 multi-year customer agreements structurally reduce Micron's cyclicality — a key bear-case risk being systematically retired
Supply constraints extend well beyond 2027; even 2028 supply improvements may not reach equilibrium
Higher memory costs are already flowing through to consumer electronics pricing (Apple), signaling broad ecosystem impact
warnings_or_risks: Beyond 2028-2030, data center buildout will shift from new construction to replacement cycles — demand growth will moderate dramatically
Historical semiconductor cyclicality: the same conditions that create booms eventually create over-supply busts
Consumer electronics AI demand boost has been modest so far; broader demand surge is still speculative

analyses.video_title: 7 Deep Value Stocks Burry is Buying Now
analyses.summary:
Daniel Pronk reviews seven stocks in Michael Burry's portfolio, giving his own independent verdict on each. The video is structured as a critical assessment — Pronk agrees with Burry on only two names (Veeva Systems and MercadoLibre) and is explicitly bearish or disinterested in the other five.

On Adobe (ADBE), Pronk notes the stock trades at 8x FCF — the cheapest since the 2009 financial crisis — with $10.3B in FCF near all-time highs. However, organic ARR growth has decelerated to 8.3% (the Semrush acquisition masks the slowdown), and both the CEO and CFO are departing. Pronk is not buying because he genuinely doesn't know where AI image/video generation will be in 5 years and ADBE is 'near the top of the list' of businesses most exposed to AI disruption. On Fiserv (FI), he sees every fundamental metric declining — revenue, margins, EPS — and is alarmed by the company raising $2.7B in debt last year to buy back shares. On Lululemon (LULU), Americas comparable sales down 6%, operating margin down to 11.2% from ~18.5%, EPS down ~40%, with no recovery projected for 2026. PE of 9 is lowest since the 2009 financial crisis, but Pronk says a low PE can look expensive in hindsight if earnings keep falling.

On Zoetis (ZTS) — down 70% from ATH, PE 12 — Pronk sees low single-digit growth and increasing competition in pet care, calling it 'not attractive.' On PayPal (PYPL), net income declined 14% (masked by share buybacks lifting EPS to -7%), active accounts flat/declining, non-GAAP EPS expected to decline further. PE 7.5 but earnings quality is deteriorating. Pronk dislikes the earnings obfuscation via per-share metrics.

The two standouts where Pronk agrees with Burry: Veeva Systems (VEEV) — 16% revenue growth, 14% projected for FY27, $8B net cash, EV/FCF 12.5x (lowest ever vs. 37x median), virtually zero capex, 20%+ FCF growth. Pronk calls this 'the highest quality company in Burry's portfolio.' MercadoLibre (MELI) — 49% YoY revenue growth to $32B TTM, 35% off ATH, owned personally by Pronk who calls it 'one of the cheapest stocks in the entire market today.'

stocks.thesis: Adobe at 8x FCF is the cheapest it has been since the 2009 financial crisis, with $10.3B in FCF near all-time highs — Burry sees the cheap multiple, but Pronk declines to buy because the AI disruption risk to creative software is too uncertain and he already uses ChatGPT instead of Photoshop.
key_points: 8x price-to-FCF — the only other time it was this cheap was March 2009 financial crisis pit
Organic ARR growth decelerated to 8.3% after excluding the Semrush acquisition; headline ARR growth of 10.2% is misleading
CEO and CFO both departed within the past year — dual leadership vacuum at a critical AI inflection point
FCF near all-time high at $10.3B — the business is profitable; the question is whether AI erodes it
Pronk is not buying — personally already substituting ChatGPT for Adobe in real workflows; AI disruption risk is 'near the top of the list' of concerns

stocks.thesis: Veeva is the highest-quality business in Burry's portfolio — 16% revenue growth, 14% FY27 guidance, $8B net cash vs $1.8B total liabilities, EV/FCF of 12.5x (lowest ever vs 37x historical median) — Pronk agrees with Burry on this one and calls it 'very interesting and looking cheap.'
key_points: EV/FCF of 12.5x vs historical median of 37x — the multiple compression happened despite continued double-digit growth
Revenue grew 16% in Q1, subscriptions 15%; FY27 guidance is 14% revenue growth and $1.6B FCF (43% margin)
Balance sheet is pristine: $8B net cash position exceeds all total liabilities; capex is virtually zero
Operating cash flow grew 20%+ YoY in Q1; stock-based compensation is only 10% of operating cash flow — unusually low for software
Vertical market software for life sciences (like Constellation/Topicus) — high switching costs, regulatory moat
CEO explicitly transitioning from 'application company to application and AI agent company' — not threatened by AI, integrating it

stocks.thesis: Pronk personally owns MercadoLibre and calls it 'one of the cheapest stocks in the entire market' — revenue +49% YoY to $32B TTM while the stock is 35% off its all-time high, creating a rare combination of elite growth and meaningful correction.
key_points: Revenue grew 49% YoY to $32B TTM — fundamentals at all-time highs while stock is in 35% correction
Pronk has been actively buying — highest personal conviction position discussed in the video
MercadoPago fintech is growing faster than the e-commerce segment — dual high-growth business units
Latin America e-commerce and digital payments penetration remains early-stage — long runway

stocks.thesis: Americas comparable sales down 6%, operating margin compressed to 11.2%, EPS down ~40% — Lululemon is projecting further deterioration in 2026 guidance; PE of 9 (lowest since 2009) but earnings are still declining, so the low multiple may not be cheap in hindsight.
key_points: Americas comparable sales declined 6% — the dominant business is losing ground to competition
Operating margin collapsed from ~18.5% to 11.2%; EPS down roughly 40% YoY
Full-year 2026 guidance calls for revenue decline of 0–1% and further EPS deterioration
PE at 9x (lowest since 2009 financial crisis) could still look expensive if earnings continue declining
China growing 34% but US market too large for international to offset domestic headwinds near-term
Pronk not interested — fashion is outside his circle of competence; views it as a turnaround story he can't evaluate

stocks.thesis: PayPal's net income declined 14% while EPS showed only -7% due to buybacks masking the real earnings deterioration; active accounts flat/declining; Pronk criticizes the company's focus on per-share metrics as obfuscating the true business decline.
key_points: Net income declined 14% YoY while non-GAAP EPS showed only -7% — $1.5B in quarterly buybacks masking profit deterioration
Active accounts essentially flat with payments per account declining — no user growth, declining engagement per user
2026 full-year non-GAAP EPS expected to be flat or slightly negative on a per-share basis (meaning actual earnings down ~7–9%)
PE 7.5x looks cheap but earnings quality is deteriorating — not a Pronk buy
Pronk criticizes transparency: company shows per-share metrics (not total earnings) to obscure how much profits are actually falling

stocks.thesis: Zoetis is down 70% from its ATH with a PE of 12 and low-single-digit projected growth — the moat is weakening (increased pet care competition, lower vet visits), and Pronk sees better quality opportunities elsewhere despite the compressed multiple.
key_points: Down 70% from ATH; PE 12 is the lowest ever vs. prior lows of 28 when growth was 13–15%
CEO cited 'broad deterioration in US business' with increased price sensitivity from pet owners and increased competition
2026 guidance: revenue +3.5%, adjusted EPS +4% — very low single-digit growth
Animal health moat (vet relationships, patents, regulatory barriers) is cited but competition is visibly eroding it

key_theses: Burry's portfolio is primarily deep value plays with very low multiples, but Pronk argues low multiples can look expensive if earnings continue declining (Fiserv, PayPal, Lululemon)
The highest quality name in Burry's portfolio is Veeva Systems — 16% growth, 12.5x EV/FCF (vs 37x median), net cash exceeds all liabilities; Pronk agrees with Burry here
MercadoLibre is the one Burry pick Pronk personally owns — 49% revenue growth while stock is 35% off ATH; calls it 'one of the cheapest stocks in the market'
Adobe's 8x FCF cheapness is real but Pronk won't buy because AI disruption risk to creative software is genuinely unknowable over 5 years
Burry's strategy appears to be: find stocks at historically cheap multiples with businesses that haven't been fully disrupted yet — a high-risk/high-reward approach
warnings_or_risks: PayPal buybacks masking real earnings decline of 14% — per-share metrics are misleading; actual business is deteriorating
Lululemon 2026 guidance projects continued revenue and EPS declines — a 9x PE is not cheap if earnings fall another 30–40%
Fiserv raised debt to buy back shares while fundamentals decline — a classic financial engineering red flag
Adobe's dual CEO/CFO departure creates governance risk at the exact moment AI disruption requires bold strategic decisions
Low multiples across Burry's portfolio may reflect rational market pricing of genuine disruption risk, not a mispricing opportunity

analyses.video_title: Should Investors Buy Celsius Stock Instead of Monster Stock?
analyses.summary: Parkev compares Celsius Holdings and Monster Beverage across financial metrics and valuation, concluding that Celsius is the better investment despite Monster's superior current profitability. Monster outperforms on virtually every operating metric — $8.8B vs $3B TTM revenue, 24.9% vs 11.18% CFO-to-sales margin, 26.25% vs 5.6% ROIC — and trades near its 52-week high of $97.86. However, Monster's forward PE of 42x vs Parkev's DCF fair value of $67 makes it overvalued. Celsius, by contrast, trades at a forward PE of just 18 near its 52-week low of $27.50, with a DCF fair value of $59 vs the current $29 price — more than 100% upside implied. Parkev acknowledges Monster deserves a premium but argues 2.3x the valuation (42x vs 18x) is excessive given the performance gap. His pick is Celsius for the better risk/reward, with international expansion (particularly in markets where Celsius has already demonstrated success) being the key growth driver that should close the profitability gap over time.

stocks.thesis: Celsius trades at 18x forward earnings near its 52-week low with a DCF fair value of $59 vs $29 market price — more than 100% upside — and international expansion into proven markets should close the profitability gap with Monster over time; clearly the better investment of the two today.
key_points: DCF fair value $59 vs $29 current price — over 100% upside implied
Forward PE of 18 vs Monster's 42 — trades at less than half the multiple despite operating in the same growing category
Trading near 52-week low of $27.50 — market sentiment extremely negative vs Monster near 52-week high
International expansion with proven market partners reduces execution risk — early markets have demonstrated success
ROIC of 5.6% currently below WACC but Parkev expects improvement as international scale builds
Energy drink category growing — Celsius is capturing market share as a premium functional beverage

stocks.thesis: Monster is the operationally superior business (24.9% CFO margin, 26.25% ROIC, near its 52-week high) but at a forward PE of 42x vs a DCF fair value of $67, the stock at $97 is overvalued by roughly 45% and offers worse risk/reward than Celsius.
key_points: DCF fair value $67 vs $97 current market price — roughly 45% overvalued
Forward PE 42x is more than twice Celsius's 18x for a company with lower growth runway
Trades near 52-week high of $97.86 — all positive sentiment already priced in
ROIC 26.25% nearly 2x WACC — excellent business quality, but the price reflects that and more

key_theses: Celsius offers 100%+ DCF upside near its 52-week low while Monster is near its 52-week high and 45% above DCF fair value — sentiment divergence creates a clear opportunity
Monster deserves a premium valuation over Celsius given its superior profitability, but not a 2.3x premium — the current gap is excessive
Celsius's international expansion into proven markets with partners reduces risk while providing the growth runway that justifies paying up relative to current profitability
warnings_or_risks: Celsius ROIC of 5.6% is below its WACC — the business must execute international expansion successfully to justify the DCF bull case
CFO-to-sales of 11.18% and declining since 2023 — profitability trend has been negative recently
Monster's operational dominance (24.9% margins, 26.25% ROIC) means Celsius has a long way to go to match the quality of the benchmark

analyses.video_title: Crypto Technical Analysis  Bitcoin  Ethereum  Solana And Alts
analyses.summary:
Gareth Soloway delivers a technical analysis sweep of the major crypto markets, combining chart pattern reading with Elliott Wave concepts. His overarching view is cautiously bearish on Bitcoin in the medium term, with selective bullishness on specific altcoins showing relative strength.

On Bitcoin, Gareth is concerned about a breakout level that was reclaimed but subsequently broken back below, which he likens to a door being knocked off its hinges — the psychological and technical damage lingers even after price recovers above the level. He expects Bitcoin to need one more meaningful leg down to flush out the weak hands, particularly noting that Michael Saylor's average cost in the low $70,000s represents a magnet for a potential squeeze. That said, he is not outright bearish for swing traders and has maintained profitable long positions throughout the year.

Solana is his top altcoin pick. He highlights the asset's long-term ascending trend line dating to 2024 as a reliable support level that has held on multiple tests, and notes exceptional relative strength versus both Bitcoin and the broader altcoin market in recent sessions. He targets a move to $83 near-term and a retest of the $97–100 zone on a broader crypto rally.

XRP presents an interesting technical setup — a well-defined descending trend line that has been tested six times. The more times a trend line is tested without breaking, the more explosive the eventual breakout tends to be. Gareth is neutral-to-cautiously optimistic; support at $1 defines the risk. Ethereum is holding its key support line better than Bitcoin. Avalanche needs to reclaim $6.38 on a daily close before becoming actionable. Sui (SUI) at ~$0.71 with a stop at $0.66 offers a defined 7% risk for potential 10-40% upside. Hyperliquid shows a potential bear-flag formation.

stocks.thesis: Technical damage from a failed breakout suggests Bitcoin needs one more leg down to flush out leveraged longs before a durable bottom forms; near-term bounces are tradeable but medium-term risk is skewed lower.
key_points: Double-bottom breakout was violated and reclaimed but left technical damage — less reliable support now
Expects three-wave structure to the downside to complete; final fear leg likely still ahead
Michael Saylor's average cost in the low $70,000s seen as a potential price target for a squeeze
Long-term bullish on Bitcoin but current chart favors swing trading bounces rather than holding through

stocks.thesis: Solana is showing superior relative strength versus Bitcoin and peers, holding a long-term ascending trend line from 2024 with repeated bounce confirmations; targets $83 near-term and $97–100 on a broader crypto rally.
key_points: Long-term ascending trend line from 2024 has been support on every test — currently tagging it again
Notably stronger price action in recent sessions compared to Bitcoin and other altcoins
Near-term resistance at $83; full target retest of the $97–100 zone
Gareth maintains a live long position in Solana

stocks.thesis: XRP has been hammered into a well-tested descending trend line six times — a spring-coil setup where an eventual breakout could be explosive, but price action has not yet confirmed the break; support sits at $1.
key_points: Descending trend line tested six times — tighter coiling implies more explosive breakout if it comes
Recent price action weaker than Solana, which is why Gareth prefers SOL over XRP currently
Key support at $1 on a descending trend line; a break below is a stop signal
No active long position taken — watching for confirmed breakout

stocks.thesis: Ethereum is holding its key horizontal support level better than Bitcoin, making it slightly preferred among the large-caps, but the broader chart remains cautiously bearish alongside the overall crypto market.
key_points: Key horizontal support line held on the recent pullback — outperforming Bitcoin on relative basis
Gareth has a partial Ethereum long after taking profits on the recent bounce
Not compelling enough to add to versus Solana on a chart basis

stocks.thesis: Sui offers a defined-risk setup at current prices (~$0.71) with a clear stop at its long-term trend line (~$0.66) and potential for 10–40% upside on a crypto relief rally.
key_points: Long-term trend line dating to 2023 provides well-defined support and stop level at ~$0.66
Risk/reward attractive: 7% downside risk for potential 10-40% upside
Chart is not strong in isolation, but defined risk parameters make it a tradeable setup

stocks.thesis: Avalanche is currently un-investable until it can reclaim $6.38 on a daily close; avoid unless that level is restored.
key_points: Significant recent decline; had a strong reversal candle but needs to reclaim $6.38 on a daily close
Current price action hitting the reclaim level from below — stay away until confirmed
No active position

key_theses: Bitcoin has technical damage from a failed breakout and likely needs one more fear-driven leg down to establish a durable bottom
Solana is the strongest chart in crypto right now with confirmed trend-line support and relative outperformance
Altcoin sentiment is at extreme bearish levels, setting up for a potential sharp relief rally similar to software stocks that were sold off for AI names
XRP's coiling descending trend line setup means a confirmed breakout would be unusually explosive
Swing trading bounces remains the preferred approach over outright long-term positioning in most altcoins
warnings_or_risks: Bitcoin's failed breakout level now acts as weaker support — bulls need to maintain price above it to prevent further selling
Michael Saylor margin-call scenario could push Bitcoin to the low $70,000s before a real bottom
Hyperliquid showing potential bear-flag formation — watch for downside breakdown
Altcoin recoveries have been quick to give back gains; sentiment improvement alone isn't sufficient without Bitcoin stabilizing

analyses.video_title: Top 5 Deep Value Stocks to Buy at 52 Week Lows
analyses.summary:
Zach from Dividend Data makes the case that the 2026 'SaaS apocalypse' — a sector-wide software valuation collapse driven by AI disruption fears — has created extreme deep value in high-quality companies whose actual business fundamentals remain sound. He draws a parallel to the 2025 'Google Search is dead' scare, which turned out to be a buying opportunity, and argues the market is again overreacting to an AI narrative.

The macro backdrop is a bifurcated market: memory-related AI beneficiaries like Micron and SanDisk are up thousands of percent and trading at 52-week highs, while software and consulting companies have been sold off 40–66% over the past year as investors fear AI will disrupt or destroy their business models. Zach's core thesis is that the market is pricing these companies as if they'll be significantly impaired within 5–10 years, yet current financials show continued revenue growth and expanding free cash flow across the board.

His five picks all share the same profile: double-digit revenue growth, accelerating free cash flow generation, historically low valuation multiples (P/FCF of 7–18x versus historical medians of 28–74x), and no evidence of fundamental business deterioration. He presents detailed metric tables for each, using a 'value graph' tool from dividenddata.com to quantify discount-to-fair-value.

For Accenture specifically, he makes an additional qualitative argument: AI consulting is itself becoming a major industry, and Accenture is well-positioned as a technology solutions firm to help large enterprises navigate the AI transition — much the way it helped them with digital transformation. He notes OpenAI and Anthropic are themselves building consulting arms, validating the opportunity. His overall message is patient, long-term and stock-selective: some software companies will not adapt and become casualties, but the five he's chosen are financially robust enough to have time to do so.
macro_topics: Memory shortage driving AI hardware winners

stocks.thesis: Down 57% due to AI consulting disruption fears, yet revenue grew 6.7% YoY and FCF jumped 22% to $12.58B; trading at 6x FCF vs. historical median P/E of 27.9x — an extreme valuation discount for a business showing no fundamental deterioration.
key_points: Revenue $73B TTM, up 6.7% YoY; 10-year CAGR of 7.89% unchanged
Free cash flow $12.58B TTM, up 22% YoY — FCF growth outpacing revenue
Market cap ~$76.6B implies roughly 6x annual FCF, far below historical norms
Trading at a P/E of 9.4 versus historical median of 27.9 — 66% below implied fair value
Dividend yield of ~5% versus historical median of 1.48%; 13.1% 5-year dividend CAGR
AI consulting is a growing industry; Accenture positioned to help enterprises navigate AI adoption

stocks.thesis: Down 43% over the past year despite continued double-digit growth and massive FCF; founder-led company with Marc Benioff at the helm and one of the largest buyback programs ever announced — third highest buybacks in the market after Nvidia.
key_points: Revenue $42.83B TTM, up 11% YoY; FCF $14.66B, up 15.9% YoY
Repurchased $37B of stock over trailing 12 months — third most in the entire market
Trades at P/E of 10.62x versus historical median of 30x — 65% below implied fair value
FCF multiple 67% below 5-year historical median
Founder-led by Marc Benioff, which Zach views as a structural advantage for adaptation

stocks.thesis: Down 66.2% over the past year yet growing revenue 15% and FCF 26% YoY; dividend yield at all-time highs relative to history; P/FCF of 9.3x versus historical median of 32.5x.
key_points: Revenue $20.93B TTM, up 15% YoY; FCF $7.7B, up 26% YoY
Dividend yield at historically extreme levels; 15% 5-year dividend CAGR
FCF payout ratio of 19.5% — dividend is very well covered
P/FCF of 9.3x versus historical median of 32.5x — 71% below implied fair value
Products include TurboTax, QuickBooks, Credit Karma, Mailchimp — diversified consumer and SMB base

stocks.thesis: Down 48.7% and described as an 'ultimate victim of the SaaS apocalypse,' yet still growing revenue 11.5% and FCF 12.6% YoY; P/FCF of 7.37x versus 5-year median of 28.12x implies 74% discount.
key_points: Revenue $25.2B TTM, up 11.5% YoY; FCF $10.63B, up 12.6% YoY
P/FCF of 7.37x versus 5-year historical median of 28.12x — 74% below implied fair value
Creative Cloud subscription transition previously drove premium multiple; now priced for obsolescence
Zach leaves AI disruption risk as an open question for viewers to evaluate

stocks.thesis: Down 53% over the past year but growing revenue 21.7% and FCF 26.2% YoY — the fastest grower of the five; P/FCF of 18.63x versus historical median of 51x implies 59% discount.
key_points: Revenue $13.96B TTM, up 21.7% YoY; FCF $4.63B, up 26.2% YoY
23.4% revenue CAGR since IPO even after the 50%+ drawdown
P/FCF of 18.63x versus 5-year median of 51x — 59% below implied fair value
Not exactly at 52-week lows but close enough to be included in the screen

key_theses: The 2026 'SaaS apocalypse' is creating generational value in software stocks — AI disruption fears are being priced in, but current fundamentals show no deterioration
All five picks trade at 59–74% discounts to their historical free cash flow multiples while continuing to grow revenue and FCF at double-digit rates
Accenture is uniquely positioned to benefit from AI adoption as enterprises need expert guidance — AI consulting itself is a massive emerging industry
Founder-led companies (Salesforce) have a structural advantage in navigating disruptive periods
This is not a blanket 'buy all software' call — it requires case-by-case analysis of adaptability and management quality
warnings_or_risks: The market may remain wrong for a long time — all five could continue selling off even if fundamentals hold
True AI disruption could eventually impair the core business models if companies fail to adapt
Some software companies will become casualties of AI disruption; stock selection matters
Accenture is a large, non-founder-led corporate — slower to adapt than founder-run peers

analyses.video_title: Silver Is Breaking Major Support (Here's My Target): Major Technical Analysis Revealed!
analyses.summary:
Gareth Soloway delivers a technically bearish precious metals outlook, focused primarily on silver which he believes is breaking a major support level and beginning the next leg lower toward $54, and potentially $50. The video is split between price target calls and technical education — specifically on his 'bouncy ball pattern' and the superiority of closes over wicks when reading support tests.

On silver, Gareth had previously flagged $120 as a topping area using a 'topping tail' candle (a bar with a long upper wick closing in the lower 25% of its range). Even after two up days and a brief pierce of the high, the topping tail remained valid because no subsequent candle closed above it. Since then, silver has traced out a classic bouncy ball pattern: each bounce from the flat support level was smaller in magnitude, progressively weakening the floor. Today's decline, down over 4%, represents what Gareth views as the breakdown of that floor. His first target is $54 based on matching prior pivot highs. He expects a bounce there but ultimately sees a move to $50.

Gareth explicitly addresses the fundamental bull case — physical deficit, inflation-adjusted undervaluation, limited paper supply — and does not dispute it. His point is that short-term price is governed by fear and greed, not fundamentals, and the technical breakdown takes precedence in the near term.

Gold is assessed as being in a wedge pattern, with support around $4,000 now, drifting toward $3,900 over the next 6 weeks as the trendline descends. A downside break from the wedge targets the $3,500–$3,600 zone, which Gareth identifies as his long-term buy level. Platinum has a support zone between $1,500–$1,600 that he will begin nibbling on a swing trade. Palladium bounced from a prior support pivot, took profits, and now the same $1,100–$1,150 zone is the next buy target. Copper is in a confirmed short-term decline after breaking an upper channel parallel that served as resistance.

stocks.thesis: Silver has broken major support after a 'bouncy ball pattern' of progressively weaker bounces; Gareth expects a move to $54 (matching pivot highs) then potentially $50, despite agreeing with the long-term fundamental bull case.
key_points: Topping tail at $120 was the initial sell signal — subsequent candles never closed above the tail's high, validating it
Bouncy ball pattern: each bounce from flat support was smaller, indicating weakening demand at that level
Down over 4% today; Gareth believes this is the start of the next leg lower
Fundamental bull case (physical deficit, inflation-adjusted undervaluation) is valid long-term but irrelevant for near-term price action
Closes are more reliable than wicks when assessing support strength — lower close takes precedence over lower wick

stocks.thesis: Gold is in a wedge pattern with support near $4,000; direction of the breakout from this wedge will determine the next move — upside break is possible, but a downside break targets the $3,500–$3,600 long-term buy zone.
stocks.price_target: $3,500–$3,600 on a wedge breakdown (long-term buy level); watching $3,900 as near-term support
key_points: Wedge support currently near $4,000, converging toward $3,900 in approximately 6 weeks
Watching for breakout direction from the wedge — no directional bias yet
Already holds long-term gold positions; would add more at $3,500–$3,600

stocks.thesis: Approaching a significant support zone at $1,500–$1,600 that encompasses multiple prior pivot lows and a gap fill; Gareth will begin nibbling a swing trade if price reaches this zone.
key_points: Zone encompasses prior pivot lows and a gap fill — multi-layered support
Will initiate swing trade on approach to the zone

stocks.thesis: Previously bought the obvious pivot support and took profits on the bounce; the same support logic now applies at $1,100–$1,150 for the next swing entry.
key_points: Prior trade executed at obvious pivot support — bought, bounced, took profits
Same zone logic applies at the next key level: $1,100–$1,150

stocks.thesis: Copper broke below an upper channel parallel that had acted as resistance repeatedly; now in confirmed short-term decline following the break.
key_points: Broke the upper channel parallel that was flagged as resistance in the prior video
Short-term decline confirmed; Gareth had this called ahead of the move

key_theses: Silver is breaking a major support floor after a textbook bouncy ball pattern — next targets are $54 then potentially $50
Short-term markets are ruled by fear and greed, not fundamentals — the physical silver deficit is real but irrelevant to the near-term price action
Gold is at a critical juncture in a wedge pattern; the breakout direction will determine the next significant move
Closes trump wicks when analyzing support — a lower close is always more meaningful than a lower intraday pierce
warnings_or_risks: Precious metals physical deficit and inflation-adjusted undervaluation remain valid long-term bull arguments — this is a trading call, not a structural bear thesis
Gold could break to the upside from the wedge pattern rather than down — Gareth acknowledges this is not directionally resolved
Silver $50 target would be a historically significant level tested in 2011 and 2019 — could see major support there

analyses.video_title: Should You Buy ASML Stock Before the Huge Investor Update?
analyses.summary:
Parkev reviews ASML ahead of the company's mid-July investor update, concluding with a Hold rating and explicitly recommending against buying before the event. The fundamental case for ASML is genuinely exceptional — 5x revenue growth since 2017 (to $39B TTM), 45% ROIC (approaching 4x its 11.63% WACC), 30.32% CFO-to-sales ratio, and a near-monopoly in EUV lithography equipment. Samsung and SK Hynix alone are planning hundreds of billions in manufacturing expansion, and ASML is essentially the only company whose equipment they can use for advanced-node production.

However, the valuation is the obstacle. At a forward PE of 52x — near the highest in its recent history — and a DCF fair value of $1,124 vs. a market price of $1,870, Parkev calculates roughly 40% downside to his intrinsic value estimate. The incredible business quality is fully and then some priced in. Parkev adds ASML to his watchlist rather than his portfolio and will reassess after the investor update.

stocks.thesis: ASML is a world-class business with near-monopoly EUV equipment, 45% ROIC at 4x WACC, and decades of semiconductor manufacturing capex tailwinds ahead — but at $1,870 vs. a $1,124 DCF fair value and a 52x forward PE, the premium is too steep to buy before the mid-July investor update.
stocks.price_target: $1,124 (DCF fair value; current price $1,870 is ~40% above fair value)
key_points: Revenue grew nearly 5x from $8B (2017) to $39B TTM — exceptional compounding over 9 years
ROIC of 45% divided by WACC of 11.63% = 3.87:1 ratio — among the best in Parkev's entire coverage universe
CFO-to-sales ratio of 30.32% (improved from 22% in 2017) — high-quality earnings conversion
Effective monopoly in EUV lithography — competitors are described as 1–3 technology steps behind, possibly unable to ever catch up
Samsung and SK Hynix planning hundreds of billions in manufacturing expansion — direct ASML equipment demand
DCF fair value $1,124 vs. $1,870 market price — ~40% downside to intrinsic value
Forward PE of 52x near the highest level since early 2024 — premium is stretched
Hold rating; won't buy before investor update; will reassess post-results

key_theses: ASML is a generational business with durable competitive advantages — but the current price fully discounts this and then some
The semiconductor manufacturing capex boom (Samsung, SK Hynix, Micron, Intel, TSMC all expanding) provides years of order visibility
The gap between ASML's quality and its valuation makes it a watchlist candidate, not a buy — wait for valuation to come to you
warnings_or_risks: Forward PE 52x near the highest in recent history — limited margin of safety at current prices
DCF fair value $1,124 implies ~40% downside if the market re-rates to intrinsic value
Investor update in mid-July is a binary event — any guidance miss at stretched valuation could cause a sharp de-rating

analyses.video_title: When to Buy MSFT Stock?
analyses.summary:
Daniel Pronk makes a strongly bullish case for Microsoft, describing it as genuinely undervalued at a decade-low valuation multiple. MSFT is down 34% from all-time highs and 25% in just the past month, driven by three overlapping bear narratives: AI will disrupt its software business, capex won't generate adequate ROI as chip prices rise, and dependence on OpenAI for Azure growth creates concentration risk. Daniel systematically dismantles each argument with financial data.

On software disruption: Microsoft 365 commercial cloud grew 15% YoY, consumer cloud 29%, LinkedIn 9%, Dynamics 365 17%, and Azure 39%. The productivity and business processes segment is at an all-time revenue high of $135B growing at 14% annually. AI revenue hit $37B annualized, up 123% YoY, with Copilot seats at 20 million (paid seats up 250% YoY). These numbers collectively refute the idea that AI is eating Microsoft's software business — it's actually driving it.

On capex ROI: Operating cash flow margin is at a decade high of 53.5% and operating cash flow hit an all-time high of $170B TTM, growing 18% annually. The capex cycle is compressing free cash flow but the underlying operations have never been more profitable. Daniel views the capex as rational given hyperscalers are universally capacity-constrained — they have demand they physically cannot capture.

On OpenAI concentration: Azure grew 39% overall; the non-OpenAI remaining performance obligations still grew 28% YoY. The CFO noted OpenAI actually gets cheap compute, meaning the OpenAI relationship is lower-margin than enterprise customers. Deleting OpenAI from the equation still leaves a rapidly growing, highly profitable cloud business.

Valuation is where Daniel is most emphatic: at a forward P/E of ~19 and P/OCF of 15.3x, Microsoft trades below its 2022 bear market lows and the 2020 COVID crash — the last similar multiple was 2017, when revenue was growing only 3%. Today revenue is accelerating. His DCF using conservative 13% OCF growth (vs. actual 18%) and 18x OCF (vs. historical median 22x) yields a fair value of $460 and a 2029 price target of $600, implying 20% CAGR. Daniel has not bought yet due to portfolio cash constraints but says he is on the verge of trimming other positions.
macro_topics: Micron earnings impact on cloud stocks

stocks.thesis: Microsoft is at a decade-low valuation (P/OCF 15.3x, forward P/E ~19) while operating cash flows grow at 18% annually and AI revenue has more than doubled YoY. All three bear arguments are contradicted by financial data. Conservative DCF implies 20% CAGR to $600 by 2029.
key_points: Down 34% from ATH, 25% in the past month — near decade-low P/OCF of 15.3x vs. 22x historical median
Operating cash flow at all-time high of $170B TTM, growing 18% annually; OCF margin at decade high of 53.5%
AI revenue $37B annualized, up 123% YoY; Copilot paid seats up 250% YoY to 20M
Azure grew 39%; non-OpenAI remaining performance obligations still grew 28% YoY
Productivity/Business Processes at all-time high of $135B revenue, growing 14%
P/OCF of 15.3x is below 2022 bear market lows (~19x) and 2020 COVID crash (~22x)
Last traded at similar multiple in 2017 when revenue was growing only 3% — now it's accelerating
Hyperscalers all say they are capacity-constrained — demand for compute clearly exceeds supply

stocks.thesis: Mentioned as context: Micron's earnings report showing elevated chip prices is causing hyperscaler stocks to sell off, as higher memory costs reduce ROI on data center capex.
key_points: Micron earnings showed prices elevated and 'higher for longer' — negative for hyperscaler ROI
Market has rotated into memory/chip stocks and away from cloud/software

key_theses: Microsoft is at a decade-low valuation while the business has never been more profitable — the market is wrong about all three bear narratives
AI is a tailwind for Microsoft's software business, not a threat: distribution plus proprietary enterprise data creates defensible moats in an AI world
Once the hyperscaler capex cycle slows, free cash flow will 'absolutely explode' as the underlying operating profitability converts
The capex spending cannot continue at 100% of OCF forever — the normalization of that cycle is a massive free cash flow catalyst
Microsoft is the highest-conviction play in enterprise software given its distribution breadth and data moat
warnings_or_risks: Rising chip prices (as confirmed by Micron earnings) reduce ROI on data center capex — a legitimate near-term concern
45% of remaining performance obligations tied to OpenAI creates concentration risk if that relationship deteriorates
Market has clearly rotated away from hyperscalers and shows no near-term sign of reversing
More Personal Computing segment (10% of profits) is declining: Windows OEM -3%, Xbox -7%

analyses.video_title: Huge News for Broadcom Stock and Nvidia Stock Investors! | AVGO Stock | NVDA Stock
analyses.summary:
Parkev covers OpenAI's announcement of its first custom AI chip, developed in partnership with Broadcom and named 'Jalapeno.' The chip reportedly delivers better performance per watt than current state-of-the-art chips in early testing, making it a credible inferencing alternative for AI workloads. Parkev frames this as expected — not a surprise — and uses it to reinforce his dual long position in both Broadcom and Nvidia.

His core argument is that Nvidia competition was inevitable and foreseeable: 75% gross margins and multi-year 50%+ revenue growth create an irresistible incentive for both competitors and customers to develop alternatives. Hyperscalers and AI labs will always want negotiating leverage against a dominant supplier. But Parkev stresses that custom chips from Broadcom and others are currently targeted at inferencing, not training — the more computationally demanding (and Nvidia-dominated) workload. Training remains Nvidia's stronghold.

For Nvidia, Parkev maintains his buy thesis: the company is still sold out of all products it produces, meaning even with Broadcom-built alternatives entering the market, total GPU demand continues to exceed supply. He would only become concerned if Nvidia loses its innovation leadership and begins to see order declines — neither of which he currently observes.

For Broadcom, the Jalapeno partnership is exactly the kind of announcement Parkev expected would continue over coming quarters. He calculates Broadcom's fair value at $496 against a current price of $381 — roughly 30% upside — and positions it as a natural portfolio complement to Nvidia: if Nvidia loses market share, it's likely losing it to Broadcom-class custom silicon partnerships. Owning both reduces idiosyncratic Nvidia risk while maintaining AI infrastructure exposure.

stocks.thesis: Broadcom is the primary beneficiary of hyperscalers building custom AI chips; the OpenAI 'Jalapeno' partnership is the first of many such announcements; fair value at $496 vs. current $381 represents ~30% upside and provides Nvidia risk mitigation.
key_points: OpenAI's 'Jalapeno' chip built with Broadcom claims better performance per watt than current best-in-class in early testing
Custom chips are for inferencing — where AI labs generate revenue — not training, making economics compelling
Parkev expects similar partnership announcements to continue over multiple quarters
Fair value calculated at $496 vs. current price of $381
Owning AVGO alongside NVDA reduces risk: if Nvidia loses share, Broadcom captures it

stocks.thesis: Nvidia remains a top-10 stock pick despite growing custom chip competition; still sold out of all production, demonstrating demand exceeds any competitive pressure; competition was expected and does not change the near-term bull case.
key_points: Nvidia is still fully sold out of all GPUs it produces — supply constraints remain the binding factor
Custom chips from hyperscalers are additive demand, not substitution, because Nvidia can't supply the full market
Competition will intensify but Nvidia's innovation leadership and training dominance are intact
Parkev would only turn bearish if Nvidia loses innovation leadership AND order volume starts declining

key_theses: OpenAI's Jalapeno chip with Broadcom validates the custom silicon trend — but this was foreseeable and priced in by informed investors
Inferencing is where AI labs generate revenue; custom chips targeting inferencing reduce costs without threatening Nvidia's training dominance
AVGO + NVDA is a complementary pair: Broadcom hedges against Nvidia's market share risk while both benefit from AI infrastructure buildout
Nvidia's competitive moat is innovation velocity and ecosystem depth, not simply current market share
warnings_or_risks: AI labs and cloud hyperscalers are actively motivated to reduce dependence on Nvidia — this trend will intensify
Custom chips could eventually expand from inferencing into training, threatening Nvidia's highest-margin workloads
AMD is also developing credible GPU alternatives that Parkev did not recommend at current 2026 valuations

analyses.video_title: Oil Just Hit My Buy Zone (I'm Buying Now): Natural Gas, Silver & Gold
analyses.summary:
Gareth Soloway announces he is actively buying oil using a dollar-cost-averaging approach as crude approaches his $67–$69 support zone (currently ~$69.50), targeting a swing trade to ~$80. He uses a 'shotgun approach' — initiating small positions at multiple support levels rather than trying to call the exact bottom. The rationale is a combination of oversold technical conditions, strategic reserve refilling demand, and the Iran nuclear deal 60-day negotiation window creating price volatility that historically produces bounces.

Longer term, Gareth remains structurally bearish on oil, targeting $55 after the bounce plays out. His bear thesis rests on two fundamentals: supply increases from Iran sanctions relief, Russian waivers, and US production pressure; and a projected slowdown in the AI capex cycle later in 2026–2027 which he argues is the primary stimulus propping up the US economy. He believes a technical retrace of the full prior up-move, combined with an eventual AI spending rollover, could push oil back toward $50 in a genuine recession.

Natural gas is the other bullish setup: a cup-and-handle / wedge pattern is forming and a break above approximately $3.35 would trigger a run toward $4.30. Gareth attributes the setup to seasonal demand front-running by institutional money ahead of the winter heating season.

On silver, he reconfirms last session's bearish call — targeting $54 before he starts accumulating. Gold is getting close to his $3,900–$4,000 bounce zone and has started to bounce, but he still expects an eventual move to $3,500–$3,600 where he would add to long-term holdings.
macro_topics: Iran nuclear deal impact on crude

stocks.company_name: Crude Oil (WTI spot / US Oil Fund)
stocks.thesis: Crude at ~$69.50 is entering Gareth's $67–$69 buy zone; he is actively DCA-ing long for a swing trade to ~$80, supported by strategic reserve refilling, Iran deal volatility, and oversold technicals. Structurally bearish to $55 after the bounce.
key_points: Currently buying in the $67–$69 zone using a shotgun/DCA approach; no attempt to pick exact bottom
Near-term bull catalysts: strategic reserve refilling, no official recession yet, Iran deal 60-day window = volatility
Swing target: bounce back to prior support low around $80 (10–15% gain)
Longer-term bear thesis: full retrace of prior up-move is technically a bare flag; AI capex slowdown in late 2026/2027 removes the economy's primary stimulus
Additional supply pressure: Iran sanctions relief, Russian waivers, US production ramp

stocks.company_name: Natural Gas (United States Natural Gas Fund)
stocks.thesis: Cup-and-handle / wedge pattern forming; a breakout above ~$3.35 triggers a run toward $4.30 driven by seasonal institutional front-running of winter heating demand.
key_points: Wedge pattern squeezing price — watching for breakout above $3.35
Seasonal demand: institutional money rotates into nat gas ahead of winter, often well before retail notices
If breakout holds, target is $4.30

stocks.thesis: Reconfirming prior bearish call; $54 is first accumulation target, consistent with prior session's analysis.
key_points: Getting close to his first buy level at $54; was calling for this decline when silver was well above $100

stocks.thesis: Bouncing off the $3,900–$4,000 support level as expected; still expects an eventual move to $3,500–$3,600 where he would add to long-term holdings.
key_points: Gold bounced at the $3,900–$4,000 zone Gareth had flagged as support
Near-term bounce in progress but longer-term bias remains toward $3,500–$3,600

key_theses: Oil is entering a short-term buy zone at $67–$69 for a swing trade to $80, supported by strategic reserves and Iran deal dynamics
AI capex is the primary stimulus keeping the US economy out of recession; a rollover in that spending late 2026/2027 would be the trigger for oil to break toward $50
Natural gas is setting up a technically strong cup-and-handle breakout with seasonal tailwinds
Short-term markets are ruled by fear and greed — commodities follow the same technical patterns as equities
warnings_or_risks: Oil could continue lower to $65 or $60 before bouncing — Gareth is sizing positions small with plans to add on weakness
An official recession declaration (two quarters negative GDP) would send oil well below $50
Iran deal may not materialize or could collapse, removing the supply pressure narrative

analyses.video_title: My Top Ranked Stock to Buy Right Now in July (2026)
analyses.summary:
This is Parkev's most comprehensive Meta bull case to date, naming it his single top-ranked stock for July 2026 with a fair value estimate of $872 versus the $561 market price — roughly 60% undervaluation. The video covers multiple dimensions of the Meta thesis in depth.

On revenue momentum: Meta's 33% revenue growth rate is the best in the Magnificent Seven excluding Nvidia, and the creator sees this as vindication of AI spending — unlike Microsoft and Amazon where capex hasn't meaningfully accelerated revenue growth, Meta's AI investments are directly improving ad impressions (+19% YoY) and average price per ad (+12% YoY) simultaneously. The AI is improving both content-to-user matching (supply side) and advertiser targeting precision (demand side), creating a compounding advantage that smaller platforms like Snap, Pinterest, Reddit, and X cannot replicate at similar scale.

On risks: the creator flags a quarter-over-quarter decline in daily active users (attributed to internet disruptions in Iran and WhatsApp restrictions in Russia), which he views as less concerning than if it were caused by competitive platform improvements. Memory costs are a real concern — CapEx guidance rose $10B to $135B midpoint solely due to memory pricing, and this squeeze affects all hyperscalers. Balance sheet risk is growing: Meta has gone from zero debt to $59B in debt over 2-3 years and free cash flow is converging toward Amazon's negative territory. Stock-based compensation jumped 50% year-over-year to $6B as AI talent competition intensifies.

Revenue per employee is at an all-time high of $2.63M, up from a $1.9M prior peak, and accelerating. Parkev views this as the most important long-term signal from Meta's AI investment. He owns the stock and plans to add more.
macro_topics: free cash flow vs capex tradeoffs

stocks.thesis: Best stock to buy in July 2026 — 60% undervaluation vs $872 fair value; AI investments are uniquely effective at widening the moat vs social media peers while simultaneously generating 33% revenue growth.
key_points: 33% revenue growth — best in Mag-7 excluding Nvidia — directly attributable to AI improving ad targeting on both content and user sides
Ad impressions up 19% YoY without changing ad load; average price per ad up 12% YoY — a rare dual tailwind
Revenue per employee at all-time high $2.63M vs prior peak $1.9M — AI productivity compounding
Smaller social platforms cannot match $125-135B annual AI investment — competitive moat widening faster than market recognizes
Key risks: CapEx up $10B to $135B midpoint due to memory prices alone; $59B in debt now from zero 3 years ago; SBC up 50%
DAU decline attributed to government-driven internet restrictions in Iran and Russia, not competitive loss — creator views this as less concerning

key_theses: Meta is uniquely using AI to enhance its existing dominant business — not just building infrastructure to rent — which creates longer-duration competitive advantage than peers
The combination of rising ad impressions AND rising price per ad simultaneously is exceptional and driven directly by AI investment returns
Fair value of $872 against a $561 price represents one of the widest risk-adjusted upside cases in large-cap tech
warnings_or_risks: Memory price increases added $10B to CapEx guidance with no near-term relief expected until at least end of 2027
Debt jumped from zero to $59B in 3 years and free cash flow is approaching negative territory like Amazon
Stock-based compensation up 50% to $6B — AI talent war is raising total compensation costs significantly
DAU declined quarter-over-quarter for the first time in a long time — even if government-driven, sets a concerning precedent

analyses.video_title: DraftKings Stock Analysis: Buy or Sell? | DKNG Stock Analysis
analyses.summary:
Parkev provides a bullish update on DraftKings (DKNG) following a 15% run over the past month, reaffirming his buy rating and arguing the recent price appreciation does not eliminate the opportunity. Despite the stock still being down over 16% in 2026, he highlights a meaningful improvement in profitability and business trajectory.

The core bull case centers on DraftKings' successful pivot into prediction markets. The company saw a 24% month-over-month increase in prediction market trading volume, reaching $1.3 billion, and has self-certified its first contracts for an in-house prediction market exchange. This expansion opens previously inaccessible markets like California, where traditional sports gaming legislation has kept DraftKings out. Parkev sees this as a net positive despite increased competition from other entrants lacking the same regulatory overhead.

Financially, Parkev emphasizes the company's shift to self-sufficiency. Cash flow to sales has climbed from -32% in 2021 to +10.9%, meaning DraftKings no longer needs to raise external capital. Operating margin has recovered from -137% at its worst to nearly breakeven at -0.3%. Analyst sentiment is supportive, with Wells Fargo and TD Cowen maintaining buy ratings at $32 and $30 price targets respectively.

Valuation is the central argument: at a forward price-to-operating-cash-flow of 13, DraftKings trades near the low end of its historical range dating back to early 2025. Parkev's updated intrinsic value calculation puts fair value at $36.62 per share, implying roughly 27% upside from the current ~$28.77 price. He updated his free cash flow estimates upward to reflect the prediction market opportunity. His conclusion is straightforward: under $30, DKNG represents a compelling entry for long-term investors.

stocks.thesis: DraftKings is undervalued at sub-$30 with an intrinsic value of $36.62; profitability has structurally improved and the prediction market pivot opens new addressable markets including California.
key_points: Cash flow to sales ratio improved dramatically from -32% in 2021 to +10.9%, making the company self-funding
Operating margin recovering from -137% at its nadir to nearly breakeven at -0.3%
Prediction market trading volume grew 24% month-over-month to $1.3 billion
Self-certified first contracts for in-house prediction market exchange
Trades at a forward P/OCF of 13, near the low end of its historical valuation range
Prediction markets give access to California, a state previously closed to DraftKings via traditional sports betting

key_theses: DKNG is still a buy under $30 despite a 15% recent run — the intrinsic value at $36.62 leaves meaningful upside
Prediction markets represent a net new growth vector that expands DraftKings' addressable market beyond traditional sports betting states
The company has crossed into self-sustaining cash flow generation, removing dilution risk
Sports gambling industry promotional wars have settled, supporting margin stability
warnings_or_risks: Low consumer stickiness — bettors have little loyalty between platforms and gravitate toward whichever offers the best promotion
Prediction markets increase competition from entrants who don't face the same state-by-state regulatory hurdles DraftKings spent years navigating
A return to heavy promotional activity industry-wide would compress margins across the sector

analyses.video_title: Roasting My Subscribers' Investment Portfolios Featuring @GrahamStephan
analyses.summary:
Humphrey Yang and Graham Stephan review three subscriber portfolios, offering feedback on asset allocation, risk tolerance, savings rates, and investment strategy. The video is primarily financial education and entertainment content rather than stock analysis.

Portfolio 1 is a 20-year-old student with $37K in assets. His Roth IRA holds VT, QQQM, and AVUV; his brokerage has small positions in SCHD and SMH (semiconductor ETF). Both creators rate his allocation as solid. They note SCHD is appropriate now given his 0% capital gains bracket but recommend transitioning away from it as income grows. They also advise against locking in a home purchase at 25 before knowing long-term plans — continue renting.

Portfolio 2 is a 28-year-old computer scientist with ~$400K in total assets including a rental property. Individual stocks include Nvidia, Google, and Meta. Graham rates it 9.8/10, Humphrey 8.0/10. Both agree the 28-year-old is likely already at coast FIRE based on the 4% rule. Too much cash relative to his income, but the rental property reserve justification brings cash back to appropriate levels. They note Google 'has been on a tear lately.'

Portfolio 3 is a 24-year-old married Texas couple with $310K in real estate and $49K in investments including IAU (gold ETF) and SLV (silver ETF) in a Roth IRA. Both creators advise replacing the precious metals with S&P 500 exposure, viewing the defensive allocation as overly conservative for a 40-year time horizon. Their concern is that gold underperforms equities long-term and the fear-driven allocation will cause underperformance.

No specific stock buy/sell recommendations are made. ETFs and stocks mentioned are those already held in subscriber portfolios, not creator picks.

stocks.thesis: Mentioned as a holding in a subscriber portfolio being reviewed — no specific buy/sell opinion given by either creator.
key_points: Subscriber portfolio holding reviewed positively as part of a diversified individual stock allocation

stocks.thesis: Mentioned as a subscriber holding; Graham noted 'Google has been on a tear lately' — passing positive comment, not a formal recommendation.
key_points: Graham Stephan noted Google has been performing well recently as an aside while reviewing subscriber holdings

key_theses: For young investors (20s-30s), aggressive growth allocations in broad index funds outperform defensive strategies like dividend stocks or precious metals over 30-40 year horizons
SCHD is appropriate in 0% capital gains tax brackets but should be transitioned out as income grows
A 28-year-old with $400K in assets is likely at coast FIRE on a $51K/year expense base — the key metric most people overlook
warnings_or_risks: Gold and silver in a Roth IRA may underperform equity allocations significantly over a 40-year time horizon
Early home purchase in a potentially non-permanent location is a financial mistake both creators agreed on
SCHD dividends become tax-inefficient once investors move into higher tax brackets

analyses.video_title: Is RocketLab Stock Financially Viable? | RKLB Stock Deep Dive Part 5
analyses.summary:
Part five of Parkev's Rocket Lab deep dive shifts from innovation narrative to financial fundamentals. He breaks down revenue by segment: Space Systems generated $137M in the most recent quarter (up 57% YoY, 32% QoQ), while Launch Services contributed $63.7M (up 79% YoY but down 16% QoQ due to fewer launches in the period). Space Systems is the larger revenue contributor today, but Launch Services is growing faster and generating more contract value — pointing to where the long-term business mix is heading.

The company ended Q1 with approximately $2.2B in total backlog, split 41.5% Launch and 58.5% Space Systems. Management expects roughly 36% of that backlog to convert to revenue within the next 12 months — approximately $800M — which aligns with a run-rate of about $200M/quarter. Parkev frames this conversion visibility as a meaningful de-risking factor for near-term revenue. Headcount in production roles grew by 250 to ~1,450, largely from personnel moving from R&D into production, signaling the transition from development to commercial scale.

Parkev is candid that Rocket Lab is early stage and that financial analysis is secondary to the innovation story at this point. He frames the investment case as a potential 5x, 10x, or even 100x opportunity given the vast and difficult-to-quantify upside of the space economy. The video closes as a teaser for a final part of the series, with the valuation verdict presumably coming next.

macro_topics.title: Space Economy Growth
macro_topics.description: The commercial space industry is in an early expansion phase with revenue opportunities that are genuinely difficult to forecast. Launch services, satellite platforms, and space systems components are all scaling simultaneously as demand from government and commercial customers accelerates.

macro_topics.title: Defense and Government Space Spending
macro_topics.description: Rocket Lab's backlog is diversified across government and commercial programs. The European defense spending surge and US government satellite programs are structural drivers of launch and space systems demand that extend well beyond near-term earnings visibility.

macro_topics.title: Reusable Launch Vehicles
macro_topics.description: The economics of the launch business are being fundamentally reshaped by reusability. Rocket Lab's progress toward reusable Neutron launches is the key swing factor in determining long-term unit economics and the addressable market size for launch services.

stocks.thesis: Rocket Lab's financials show accelerating revenue in both segments with $2.2B in backlog providing ~11 quarters of revenue visibility, and the broader space economy thesis supports a potential multi-bagger outcome over a long horizon.
key_points: Space Systems revenue of $137M in most recent quarter — up 57% YoY and 32% QoQ
Launch Services revenue of $63.7M — up 79% YoY though down 16% QoQ on fewer launches
Total backlog of $2.2B with ~36% expected to convert to revenue in next 12 months (~$800M)
Backlog is diversifying toward launch contracts, which now exceed space systems in new contract value
Production headcount grew 250 to ~1,450 as R&D personnel shift into manufacturing — signals scaling
Management frames 5x, 10x, or even 100x as plausible long-term outcomes given space economy potential
Financial viability confirmed — company is generating meaningful and growing revenue from both segments

key_theses: Rocket Lab's financials are viable and improving — both segments growing strongly year-over-year with multi-year backlog
The $2.2B backlog provides exceptional revenue visibility (~11 quarters) that is rare for an early-stage growth company
The shift from R&D to production headcount signals the company is crossing the commercialization threshold
Long-term upside is difficult to quantify but potentially enormous given the nascent state of the space economy
warnings_or_risks: Launch Services revenue fell 16% quarter-over-quarter due to launch cadence variability — revenue is inherently lumpy
Still an early-stage company where execution risk on Neutron and reusability is high
No valuation or fair value given in this part — financial viability confirmed but whether the current price is attractive is deferred

analyses.video_title: Why Is Chewy Stock Falling, and is it a Buying Opportunity? | CHWY Stock Analysis
analyses.summary:
Parkev examines Chewy following a 43% year-to-date decline and a guidance cut, concluding the stock represents a compelling buying opportunity for long-term investors willing to tolerate near-term consumer spending headwinds. The company lowered its fiscal 2026 sales outlook to $13.4-13.5 billion, citing a challenging macro environment — a pattern Parkev has noted broadly as the US consumer faces compressed disposable income from lingering inflation, tariff-driven cost increases, and elevated oil prices.

Despite the downward revision, Q1 results showed net sales rising 7.7% year-over-year and an improved gross profit margin, which Parkev attributes to what he views as a competent management team navigating a difficult environment. He notes that Chewy's more necessity-driven categories — pet food and medicine — should prove relatively resilient even as discretionary pet products like toys and accessories soften. However, investment banks have issued multiple downgrades, citing skepticism around Chewy's international expansion prospects, drawing parallels to failed overseas attempts by Best Buy, eBay, Walmart, and Target.

Parkev highlights Chewy's improving margin trajectory as the key fundamental story: operating cash-flow-to-sales ratio has grown from negative 3.8% in 2017 to positive 5.5% in 2025, and operating margins have moved from -16% to +2% over the same period. While the absolute margin levels remain modest, the direction and consistency impress him. He emphasizes that Chewy's capital-intensive fulfillment model precludes the very high margins of asset-light peers but creates a durable competitive moat that is hard to replicate at scale.

On valuation, Parkev's DCF model puts fair value at $34 against a current price of approximately $19, near its 52-week low of $18.22 versus a 52-week high of $43.84. On forward P/E of 12.1x and forward price-to-operating-cash-flow of 9.3x, he considers the stock clearly undervalued. His buy threshold is any price below $25 per share.

stocks.thesis: At roughly $19, Chewy trades near its 52-week low at a forward P/E of 12.1x and well below Parkev's DCF fair value of $34, with consistent margin improvement and a resilient core pet-essentials business that should weather the consumer spending slowdown better than discretionary peers.
key_points: Stock is down 43% year-to-date in 2026, trading near its 52-week low of $18.22
Q1 net sales grew 7.7% year-over-year, above expectations, with gross margin improvement
Management lowered FY2026 revenue outlook to $13.4-13.5B citing macro headwinds, particularly tariff-driven consumer spending compression
Operating cash-flow margin has improved from -3.8% in 2017 to +5.5% in 2025; operating margin went from -16% to +2%
Forward P/E of 12.1x and forward price-to-operating-cash-flow of 9.3x — both appear cheap relative to peers
Capital-intensive fulfillment model limits peak margins but builds a durable competitive moat
Near-term headwinds expected to persist through 2026 as consumer disposable income remains pressured
Investment banks have issued downgrades citing weak international expansion prospects

key_theses: Chewy is a buying opportunity at current prices below $25, with the stock near a 52-week low and a DCF fair value of $34
Core pet-necessity categories (food, medicine) provide demand resilience even as discretionary pet spending softens
A decade of consistent margin improvement demonstrates management competence despite challenging conditions
The capital-intensive model creates competitive durability that asset-light e-commerce rivals cannot easily replicate
warnings_or_risks: Consumer disposable income will remain pressured through 2026 due to tariffs and elevated oil prices — near-term sales headwinds are real

analyses.video_title: Ripe Underdog Stocks to BUY on the DIP ⚡️ Lighting Round Analysis
analyses.summary:
Josh opens with macro context: a red day caused by a crash in Korea's stock market, driven by over-extended bullish positioning where up to 95–98% of participants were long. He interprets institutional selling of puts at the 720–722 strike on the S&P as a signal that smart money does not expect the market to fall to those levels — a constructive read on the dip.

The video then runs a quick lightning round of stocks, themed around technically oversold 'underdog' names that Josh believes are setting up for reversal. Nike has reached a previously flagged re-entry zone below $42 and is flagged as a range trade candidate targeting a retest of $47. Salesforce hit the 59.8% drawdown level that Josh had identified as the extreme downside scenario, making it one of his top underdog picks. Microsoft is increasingly attractive, with Josh noting buyers today are getting better value than at the 2022 lows on certain metrics.

Netflix receives the most technical detail: despite breaking below $75 support and reaching a new 52-week low, Josh identifies bullish RSI divergence on the weekly timeframe and a return to the prior all-time high resistance zone ($65–$75) that should now act as support. He draws an explicit parallel to Meta falling to $80 before its massive recovery. He acknowledges the bear case — potential AWS cost escalation from $1B to $4B due to AI memory constraints — but frames the technical setup as a risk worth taking. He notes Marjorie Taylor Greene has been buying Netflix and ServiceNow.

Palantir is briefly mentioned as hitting its 52-week low again with no directional call. SpaceX is flagged as a stock to watch above its 9 EMA on the 1-hour chart, with a note to avoid being caught in a short squeeze if price recovers above that level.

analyses.video_title: Should Investors Buy Broadcom Stock Instead of Marvell Stock? | AVGO Stock | MRVL Stock
analyses.summary:
Parkev runs a head-to-head comparison of Broadcom and Marvell Technology across revenue, profitability, and valuation, concluding that Broadcom is the clear winner on every dimension and is his preferred holding. Both companies are beneficiaries of AI-driven semiconductor demand — Broadcom through its custom ASIC work with hyperscalers (notably Alphabet's TPU program) and networking equipment, Marvell through its own custom chip designs — but Parkev finds the risk-reward tilted heavily in Broadcom's favor.

On revenue scale, Broadcom dominates: $64B in fiscal 2025 revenue versus Marvell's $8.2B, with analysts projecting Broadcom reaching $228B by 2028 while Marvell reaches $22.9B by fiscal 2029. Marvell carries a higher percentage growth rate, but Broadcom adds more absolute revenue. Profitability metrics also favor Broadcom across the board: 44.5% cash flow from operations margin versus Marvell's 23.6%, and 20.3% ROIC versus Marvell's 12.79%. Broadcom's management has also guided toward further margin expansion as VMware acquisition digestion completes, with CFO-to-sales expected to return above 50%.

The valuation comparison is where Parkev's conviction becomes most pronounced. Marvell trades at a forward P/E of 66 — near the highest in its recent history — while Broadcom trades at 31.5x, near the lower end of its recent range. His DCF models put Broadcom's fair value at ~$499 versus a market price of ~$367 (36% upside), and Marvell's fair value at ~$174 versus a market price of ~$267 (35% downside). Parkev attributes much of Marvell's premium to Jensen Huang's comment that Marvell could become a trillion-dollar company, which he views as enthusiasm rather than fundamentals.

He explicitly owns Broadcom in his personal portfolio and does not own Marvell, calling this one of the easier head-to-head calls he's made — Broadcom wins on both performance and valuation simultaneously. He also briefly touches on the broader semiconductor cyclicality debate, noting that multi-year strategic agreements (referencing Micron's recent announcements) may structurally dampen cycles going forward, though the thesis remains unproven.

stocks.thesis: Broadcom outperforms Marvell on every metric — revenue scale, profitability, and valuation — with a DCF fair value of ~$499 versus a ~$367 market price, making it the clear pick of the two; creator owns it personally.
key_points: Generated $64B in revenue in fiscal 2025, projected to reach $228B by 2028 — superior absolute growth to Marvell
44.5% cash flow from operations margin and 20.3% ROIC — both meaningfully above Marvell's metrics
Forward P/E of 31.5x sits near the lower end of its recent historical range — relatively inexpensive for a high-quality compounder
Management guiding margins above 50% once VMware integration is fully absorbed
Key hyperscaler relationship: builds Alphabet's TPU tensor processing units and networking equipment
DCF fair value ~$499 vs. ~$367 market price — roughly 36% upside; creator actively looking to buy more

stocks.thesis: Marvell trades at a forward P/E of 66 (near historical highs) while its DCF fair value of ~$174 sits 35% below the market price of ~$267 — enthusiasm around Jensen Huang's trillion-dollar comment has pushed valuation well past fundamentals.
key_points: Forward P/E of 66 is near the highest valuation level in Marvell's recent history — significantly overvalued versus Broadcom's 31.5x
DCF fair value of ~$174 implies roughly 35% downside from the ~$267 market price
23.6% CFO-to-sales margin and 12.79% ROIC both trail Broadcom by a wide margin

Creator does not own Marvell and would not buy at current prices

key_theses: Broadcom wins on every axis simultaneously — better margins, better ROIC, bigger revenue, and cheaper valuation — making the choice straightforward
Marvell's forward P/E of 66 embeds significant growth optimism that its fundamentals don't yet justify; the Jensen Huang halo effect has driven sentiment beyond fair value
Broadcom's ~$499 DCF fair value vs. ~$367 market price represents a compelling 36% margin of safety in a high-quality AI infrastructure play

warnings_or_risks: Semiconductor industry remains historically cyclical — multi-year contract thesis may only delay rather than eliminate the downcycle
Broadcom's margin expansion thesis depends on VMware integration completing smoothly — any integration friction could delay the 50%+ target
